verses.flow: Now it came to pass in the thirtieth year, in the fourth month, on the fifth day of the month, as I was among the exiles by the river Chebar, the heavens were opened and I saw visions of God (Elohim).
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "thirtieth year" is most plausibly Ezekiel's own age — thirty being the year of priestly inauguration for temple service (Num 4:3), making the vision a divine commissioning in lieu of the temple ministry Ezekiel was exiled from. The river Chebar (nahr kabaru) is attested in Babylonian cuneiform texts as a major irrigation canal near Nippur, southeast of Babylon. THEOLOGICAL: "Heavens were opened" (niptchu hashamayim) signals a throne-room theophany, a pattern repeated at Christ's baptism (Matt 3:16) and Stephen's martyrdom (Acts 7:56).

verses.flow: On the fifth day of the month — it was the fifth year of the exile of King Jehoiachin —

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: the word of YHWH came expressly to Ezekiel the priest, son of Buzi, in the land of the Chaldeans by the river Chebar; and the hand of YHWH was upon him there.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Came expressly" translates the Hebrew infinitive absolute + finite verb construction (hayo hayah), emphasizing the certainty and fullness of the prophetic word. "The hand of YHWH" (yad-YHWH) is a recurring Ezekielian formula (Ezek 3:14, 22; 8:1; 33:22; 37:1; 40:1) denoting the overwhelming, almost physical compulsion of prophetic inspiration. TYPOLOGY: Ezekiel, a priest-turned-prophet in Babylonian exile, prefigures Christ who unites the offices of priest and prophet outside the sanctuary (Heb 7:11-17).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: And I looked, and behold, a storm wind (ruach se'arah) was coming from the north, a great cloud with fire flashing forth continually, and a brightness was around it, and from the midst of it, as it were gleaming metal (hashmal) from the midst of the fire.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The storm wind from the north (tsaphon) carries deliberate theological weight — in Canaanite mythology, Baal's divine mountain was in the north (Zaphon); YHWH's theophany from the north signals his sovereign supremacy over all rival divine claims (cf. Ps 48:2). HEBREW: "Hashmal" is one of the most debated terms in biblical Hebrew; it occurs only in Ezekiel (1:4, 27; 8:2) and may denote electrum (a gold-silver alloy) or a supernatural luminous substance; the LXX renders it "electron" and later Kabbalistic tradition associated it with mystical fire.

verses.flow: And from the midst of it came the likeness of four living creatures (chayyot). And this was their appearance: they had the likeness of a man (adam).
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The four "living creatures" (chayyot) represent the highest order of the divine council's heavenly throne guardians. They are later identified as cherubim (Ezek 10:20), cognate to the Akkadian "karibu" — divine intercessors who flanked royal and divine thrones throughout the ANE. Their four-faced form encapsulates the totality of the animate creation: human (intelligence), lion (wild majesty), ox (domestic strength), eagle (aerial sovereignty) — a cosmic throne guard. TYPOLOGY: The four faces were associated by the church fathers with the four Gospels: Matthew (man/king), Mark (lion), Luke (ox), John (eagle) (Irenaeus, Adv. Haer. 3.11.8).

verses.flow: And each had four faces, and each of them had four wings.

verses.flow: And their legs were straight legs, and the sole of their feet was like the sole of a calf's foot; and they sparkled like the gleam of burnished bronze.
And under their wings on their four sides they had human hands; and all four of them had their faces and their wings thus:

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: their wings touched one another; they did not turn as they moved; each went straight forward.

As for the likeness of their faces: the face of a man, and the face of a lion on the right side — for the four of them; and the face of an ox on the left side — for the four of them; and the face of an eagle — for the four of them.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The four faces — man, lion, ox, eagle — parallel the four standards of the Israelite camp in Numbers 2 as interpreted in rabbinic tradition (Num. Rab. 2:7): Reuben (man), Judah (lion), Ephraim (ox), Dan (eagle). This cosmic symmetry suggests the heavenly army mirrors the wilderness camp of YHWH's earthly people. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Such composite divine beings are well attested in ANE iconography: Mesopotamian lamassu (human-headed winged bulls) and Egyptian sphinxes served as divine throne guardians, indicating that Ezekiel's audience would have immediately recognized the visual vocabulary of a divine king's throne room.

verses.flow: Such were their faces. And their wings were spread out above; each creature had two wings touching the wing of another, and two wings covering their bodies.

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: And each went straight forward; wherever the spirit (ruach) was to go, they went, without turning as they went.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Wherever the spirit (ruach) was to go, they went" — the same ruach that animates creation (Gen 1:2) and the prophets directs the movement of YHWH's throne chariot. The total responsiveness of the chayyot to the ruach underscores the absolute sovereignty of the divine will operating through spiritual intermediaries. THEOLOGICAL: This verse becomes foundational for the later Jewish mystical tradition of Merkabah ("chariot") mysticism, which treated Ezekiel 1 as the supreme vision of the divine throne accessible only to advanced initiates (cf. Talmud Chagigah 13a).

verses.flow: As for the likeness of the living creatures, their appearance was like burning coals of fire, like the appearance of torches moving to and fro among the living creatures; and the fire was bright, and from the fire went forth lightning.
And the living creatures darted to and fro, like the appearance of a flash of lightning.

verses.flow: Now as I looked at the living creatures, behold, one wheel (ophan) was on the earth beside the living creatures, one for each of the four of them.

As for the appearance of the wheels and their construction: their appearance was like gleaming beryl. And the four of them had the same likeness, their appearance and construction being as it were a wheel within a wheel.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Wheel within a wheel" (ophan betoch ophan) — the Hebrew ophan denotes a wheel or circular form. The intersecting perpendicular wheels allow omnidirectional movement without turning, symbolizing YHWH's absolute freedom of movement across all dimensions of creation. "Gleaming beryl" (tarshish) — possibly yellow chrysolite or topaz; the same stone appears in the high priest's breastplate (Exod 28:20) and in Daniel's river-vision (Dan 10:6), linking throne-room imagery across prophetic books.

verses.flow: When they moved, they went in any of their four directions without turning as they moved.
And their rims were tall and awesome, and the rims of all four were full of eyes all around.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The wheels "full of eyes all around" (mele'ot enayim) signify omniscience — the divine chariot-throne sees in all directions simultaneously. This imagery recurs in Revelation 4:6-8 where the four living creatures are "full of eyes in front and behind," establishing the continuity of the heavenly throne room vision across the canon. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The eyes on the wheels parallel the "seven eyes of YHWH that range through the whole earth" in Zechariah 4:10 and the seven spirits before the throne in Revelation 1:4, all expressing YHWH's total surveillance of creation.

verses.flow: And when the living creatures moved, the wheels moved beside them; and when the living creatures were lifted up from the earth, the wheels were lifted up.
Wherever the spirit (ruach) was to go, they went, and the wheels were lifted up beside them, for the spirit of the living creatures was in the wheels.

verses.flow: When those moved, these moved; and when those stood still, these stood still; and when those were lifted up from the earth, the wheels were lifted up beside them, for the spirit of the living creatures was in the wheels.
Over the heads of the living creatures there was the likeness of an expanse (raqia), shining like awe-inspiring crystal, spread out above their heads.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Expanse" (raqia) directly echoes Genesis 1:6-8, the firmament of creation. Ezekiel's use of raqia above the chayyot places the divine throne at the apex of the created order, above the heavens. "Awe-inspiring crystal" (nora haqerach) — the term qerach means ice or crystal, suggesting a dazzling, transparent solidity; "nora" (awesome, fear-inspiring) is a divine attribute term (cf. Ps 68:35). THEOLOGICAL: The deliberate reuse of creation vocabulary signals that the Merkabah vision is a new-creation theophany — YHWH enthroned above his recreated cosmos, with implications for the new creation theology of the NT (2 Cor 5:17; Rev 21:1).

verses.flow: And beneath the expanse their wings were stretched out straight, one toward another; and each creature had two wings covering its body on this side and two wings covering its body on that side.

verses.flow: And when they moved, I heard the sound of their wings like the sound of many waters, like the sound of the Almighty (Shaddai), a sound of tumult like the sound of a camp; when they stood still, they let down their wings.

verses.flow: And there came a voice from above the expanse over their heads; when they stood still, they let down their wings.

And above the expanse over their heads there was the likeness of a throne (kisse), in appearance like sapphire stone; and seated above the likeness of the throne was a likeness with the appearance of a man (adam) above it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The likeness of a man" (demut kemareh adam) seated on the heavenly throne is one of the most theologically charged phrases in the Hebrew Bible. Ezekiel multiplies the language of approximation (likeness, appearance, as it were) to convey the uncanny: a human form occupying the divine throne. This is the "Ancient of Days" theophany tradition (Dan 7:9-14) and stands as the strongest OT precedent for the Incarnation — the eternal Son who is both the image of God and truly man (Col 1:15; Phil 2:6-8). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The enthroned human figure above the divine council's cherubim represents YHWH's kabod (glory) in its most direct theophanic manifestation, the "Angel of YHWH" tradition that Second Temple Judaism and early Christianity identified with the pre-incarnate Christ.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: And I saw as it were gleaming metal (hashmal), as the appearance of fire enclosed all around from the appearance of his loins and upward; and from the appearance of his loins and downward I saw as it were the appearance of fire, and there was brightness around him.

verses.flow: Like the appearance of the bow that is in the cloud on the day of rain, so was the appearance of the brightness all around. Such was the appearance of the likeness of the glory (kavod) of YHWH. And when I saw it, I fell upon my face, and I heard the voice of one speaking.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Glory" (kavod) derives from the root meaning "weight" or "heaviness" — that which has substance, honor, and gravitas. The kavod of YHWH is the visible, luminous manifestation of his presence and was the object of Israel's cultic hope (Exod 40:34-35). The rainbow (qeshet) surrounding the glory deliberately echoes the Noahic covenant sign (Gen 9:13-16), framing this terrifying vision within the context of divine faithfulness. TYPOLOGY: Ezekiel's prostration before the enthroned man-figure mirrors John's identical response before the glorified Christ in Revelation 1:17, and the kavod that has departed from Jerusalem (Ezek 10-11) is the same glory that returns in the person of Christ (John 1:14: "we beheld his glory") and will fill the eschatological temple (Ezek 43:4-5; Rev 21:22-23).

verses.flow: And He said to me, "Son of man (ben-adam), stand upon your feet, and I will speak with you."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ben-adam" (Strong's H1121 + H120) literally means "son of man" or "son of Adam," emphasizing Ezekiel's creaturely humanity before the divine glory just witnessed in chapter 1. This title occurs over 90 times in Ezekiel, far more than in any other book, grounding the prophet's identity in his mortal, Adamic nature even as he is commissioned for transcendent vision. The same title appears in Dan 7:13 for a heavenly figure, creating a typological contrast: where Ezekiel the son of man is lifted to see God's glory, the Son of Man in Daniel comes with the clouds to receive it. (Dan 7:13, Matt 8:20)

verses.flow: And ruach (ruach) entered into me as He spoke to me and set me upon my feet, and I heard Him speaking to me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The ruach (Spirit, Strong's H7307) entering Ezekiel to enable him to stand parallels the animating breath of God in Gen 2:7 and the Spirit's role in resurrection in Ezek 37. The prophet is not capable of standing before YHWH in his own strength; divine empowerment precedes divine commission. This pattern of Spirit-enablement before prophetic speech is foundational to biblical prophetology. (Ezek 37:10, Acts 2:4)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And He said to me, "Son of man (ben-adam), I am sending you to the children of Israel, to nations of rebels who have rebelled against Me; they and their fathers have transgressed against Me to this very day.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Ezekiel is commissioned to "nations" (goyim, H1471) within Israel — a striking designation applying to ethnic Israel the language normally reserved for pagan peoples. This rhetorical move signals that covenant Israel has become functionally pagan in her rebellion (mered, H4805). The exile setting (Tel-abib, 3:15) means Ezekiel speaks to a displaced community whose rebellion preceded and caused their deportation under Nebuchadnezzar in 597 B.C.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: And the children are impudent of face and hard of heart — I am sending you to them, and you shall say to them, 'Thus says the Lord YHWH.'

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: And they — whether they hear or whether they refuse, for they are a house of rebellion — yet they will know that a prophet has been among them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "house of rebellion" (beit meri, H1004 + H4805) becomes a defining refrain throughout Ezekiel's commission (vv. 5, 6, 7, 8; 3:9, 26, 27; 12:2–3). YHWH does not guarantee success in terms of audience response — only in terms of faithful delivery. The purpose of the prophetic word is that Israel "will know" (yada', H3045) that a prophet was among them, establishing divine accountability regardless of reception. (Matt 13:14–15, Rom 10:16)

verses.flow: And you, son of man (ben-adam), do not fear them, and do not fear their words, though briers and thorns are with you and you dwell among scorpions; do not fear their words and do not be dismayed at their faces, for they are a house of rebellion.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Briers and thorns" (sallonim u'sarbanim) are rare terms appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible, likely denoting painful, prickling vegetation used metaphorically for hostile people. "Scorpions" (aqrabim, H6137) intensifies the imagery of dangerous opposition. The threefold "do not fear" (al-tira') signals that courage is not the absence of danger but obedience in its presence. Compare YHWH's charge to Joshua: "Be strong and courageous." (Josh 1:9, Matt 10:28)

words.m: conjunction + verb piel perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: And you shall speak My words to them, whether they hear or whether they refuse, for they are a house of rebellion.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: But you, son of man (ben-adam), hear what I am speaking to you — do not be rebellious like that rebellious house; open your mouth and eat what I am giving to you."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The command to "open your mouth and eat" what YHWH gives anticipates the scroll-eating in 3:1–3, which is itself a vivid type of total internalization of the divine word. The prophet must consume the word before he can deliver it — a pattern fulfilled in the New Covenant where the Spirit writes the law on the heart (Jer 31:33). The Apostle John's parallel eating of the scroll (Rev 10:9–10) confirms the typological trajectory of this commissioning act. (Jer 31:33, Rev 10:9)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: And I looked, and behold, a hand was stretched out to me, and behold, in it was a scroll of a book.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: A "hand stretched out" (yad shlucha) to the prophet carrying a heavenly document echoes the mechanics of the divine council's communication with its envoys — divine messengers and prophets receive sealed or inscribed commissions from the heavenly court. Compare the written accusations in Zech 5:1–4 and the books opened in Dan 7:10. The scroll mediates between the heavenly assembly's decrees and earthly proclamation. (Dan 7:10, Zech 5:1, Rev 5:1)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And He spread it before me, and it was written on the front and on the back; and written upon it were lamentations and mourning and woe.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: That the scroll is written "on the front and on the back" (panim ve'achor) signifies the fullness and completeness of divine judgment — there is no room to add more, and no portion is left blank. "Lamentations (qinot), mourning (hegeh), and woe (hi)" form a triad of grief that summarizes the content of Ezekiel's entire message through chapter 32. This total inscription of sorrow stands in ironic contrast to the scroll's taste of honey in 3:3, pointing to the paradox that the word of YHWH, however bitter in content, is sweet in reception by the faithful. (Rev 5:1, Ezek 3:3, Rev 10:10)

verses.flow: And he said to me, "Son of man, feed your belly and fill your stomach with this scroll that I give you." And I ate it, and it was in my mouth like honey (devash) for sweetness.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word translated "sweetness" is מָתוֹק (matok, H4966), meaning sweet or pleasant. That the scroll — filled with laments and woe — tasted like honey (devash, H1706) evokes Psalm 119:103 and anticipates John's eating of the scroll in Revelation 10:9-10. The sweetness reflects the divine origin and authority of the word itself, not its content. TYPOLOGY: The ingesting of the divine word as a commissioning act echoes Jeremiah 15:16 and is consummated in Christ, the Logos made flesh (John 1:14), who is himself the living Word of God.

"For you are not sent to a people of foreign lip and heavy tongue, but to the house of Israel —"

words.m: verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: "not to many peoples of foreign lip and heavy tongue, whose words you cannot understand. Surely, if I sent you to them, they would listen to you."
"But the house of Israel will not be willing to listen to you, for they are not willing to listen to me; for all the house of Israel are of hard forehead and of stubborn heart."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "hard forehead and stubborn heart" (chazaq-metsach u'qesheh-lev) describes Israel's covenant faithlessness in somatic terms — the body itself manifesting spiritual rebellion. This language parallels Deuteronomy 31:27 and Jeremiah 5:3 and stands in contrast to the promised "heart of flesh" in Ezekiel 36:26, pointing forward to the new covenant transformation wrought by the Spirit.

verses.flow: Then the ruach (spirit/wind) lifted me up, and I heard behind me the sound of a great rushing — "Blessed be the glory (kavod) of YHWH from his place!" —
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The doxology "Blessed be the glory (kavod) of YHWH from his place" is a liturgical acclamation that has become central to Jewish synagogue worship (the Kedushah). The kavod (H3519) is YHWH's weighty, manifest divine presence — the same throne-chariot (merkavah) vision of chapter 1. The ruach (H7307) that transports the prophet is a divine agent of movement, linking the heavenly council's activity to prophetic commissioning. HISTORICAL: Second Temple mystical traditions (merkavah mysticism) regarded this verse as one of the most guarded texts; public exposition was restricted per Mishnah Chagigah 2:1.

verses.flow: and the sound of the wings of the living creatures (hayyot) touching one another, and the sound of the wheels (ophannim) beside them, even the sound of a great rushing.
verses.note: HEBREW: The "living creatures" (hayyot, H2416) and "wheels" (ophannim, H212) from the throne-chariot vision of chapter 1 reappear here audibly, connecting Ezekiel's commissioning to his foundational vision of divine sovereignty. The sound of a "great rushing" (qol ra'ash gadol) echoes the overwhelming, irresistible approach of divine presence and appears again at Pentecost (Acts 2:2), linking Spirit-empowered prophetic commission across the covenants.

verses.flow: And I came to the exiles at Tel-abib, who were dwelling by the river Chebar, and I sat where they were sitting. And I sat there stunned among them for seven days.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Tel-abib (Tel Aviv, meaning "mound of the flood/spring") was a Jewish deportee settlement on the Chebar canal in Babylonia, likely near Nippur. Archaeological evidence from Nippur includes cuneiform tablets listing Jewish names among the Babylonian population of this period. The seven days of sitting stunned (shamem) among the exiles mirrors the traditional mourning period and signals the weight of the prophetic burden laid on Ezekiel.

verses.flow: "Son of man, I have made you a watchman (tsopheh) for the house of Israel. Whenever you hear a word from my mouth, you shall warn them from me."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The office of "watchman" (tsopheh, H6822) is a royal and military metaphor: the prophet stands on the walls of the covenant community to sound warning of approaching divine judgment. This commission is repeated with expansion in Ezekiel 33:1-9, forming a structural bracket around the oracles of judgment in chapters 4-32. The watchman's accountability before YHWH for the blood of the unwarned establishes a theology of prophetic moral responsibility that runs through the entire book.

verses.flow: "When I say to the wicked (rasha), 'You shall surely die,' and you do not warn him, and you do not speak to warn the wicked from his wicked way, to save his life — that wicked person shall die in his iniquity (avon), but his blood I will require from your hand."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "his blood I will require from your hand" (damav mi'yadkha 'evaqesh) invokes the ancient covenant language of blood-guilt (Genesis 9:5; 42:22). The prophet's silence in the face of divine warning renders him complicit in the death of the unwarned sinner. This verse undergirds a robust theology of proclamation — the preacher is not merely a communicator but a covenant sentinel whose silence has lethal moral consequence.

verses.flow: "But if you warn the wicked and he does not turn from his wickedness and from his wicked way, he shall die in his iniquity, but you will have delivered your soul (nephesh)."

verses.flow: "And when a righteous person turns from his righteousness (tsedaqah) and does injustice, and I lay a stumbling block before him, he shall die. Because you have not warned him, he shall die in his sin, and his righteous deeds that he has done shall not be remembered, but his blood I will require from your hand."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse introduces the possibility of a righteous person's apostasy leading to their death, a theme that Ezekiel develops extensively (18:24; 33:12-13). The phrase "stumbling block" (mikhshol, H4383) placed by YHWH before the apostate reflects divine sovereign governance of moral retribution — God is not the author of evil but ordains the consequences of covenant abandonment. This does not contradict NT perseverance theology but addresses the covenantal standing of individuals within the national community.

verses.flow: "But if you warn the righteous person not to sin, and he does not sin, he shall surely live, because he was warned, and you will have delivered your soul."

verses.flow: And the hand of YHWH was upon me there, and he said to me, "Arise, go out into the valley (biq'ah), and there I will speak with you."
verses.note: HEBREW: The "valley" (biq'ah, H1237) is a broad, flat plain or valley floor — the same term used for the Valley of Dry Bones in Ezekiel 37:1. The reappearance of the kavod of YHWH outside the settlement, in an open space, signals that divine presence is not confined to temple or city and continues to follow the exiled community. This is a profound theological statement to a people who feared YHWH had abandoned them in Babylon.

verses.flow: "And your tongue I will cause to cleave to the roof of your mouth, so that you shall be mute and not be to them a man who rebukes, for they are a house of rebellion."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The enforced muteness of the prophet (cf. Ezekiel 24:27; 33:22) is a sign-act in itself — YHWH silences his messenger as a form of judgment upon those who will not hear. Ezekiel will not be the people's counsel or advocate; he will speak only when YHWH opens his mouth. This sovereign control of prophetic speech underscores that the word belongs entirely to YHWH, not to the prophet, a principle the Apostle Peter echoes in 2 Peter 1:20-21.

verses.flow: And you, son of man, take for yourself a brick and set it before you, and engrave upon it a city — Jerusalem.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word rendered "engrave" is chaqah (H2707), meaning to cut in, inscribe, or portray — the same root used in Ezek 23:14. The use of a clay brick (levenah, H3843) is deeply significant in an ANE context; HISTORICAL: clay tablets and bricks were the standard medium for architectural and military plans in Mesopotamia, making this symbolic act immediately intelligible to Ezekiel's exilic audience in Babylon, who would have seen such military blueprints.

verses.flow: And you, take for yourself an iron griddle, and set it as an iron wall between you and the city, and fix your face toward it, and it shall be under siege, and you shall besiege it — this is a sign ('ot) for the house of Israel.
verses.note: HEBREW: The iron griddle (machavat barzel, H4227 + H1270) elsewhere refers to a flat baking pan (Lev 2:5), here repurposed as a wall of iron, symbolizing the impenetrable divine judgment separating YHWH from Jerusalem. THEOLOGICAL: The term 'ot (H226), sign, indicates the acted prophetic sign (mimed prophecy) as a covenantally binding declaration — what the prophet enacts in symbol, YHWH enacts in history. TYPOLOGY: The iron wall anticipates the theme of divine hiddenness during judgment, echoed in Isaiah 59:2 and the NT concept of divine abandonment (Matt 27:46).

verses.flow: And you, lie upon your left side, and place upon it the iniquity ('avon) of the house of Israel; the number of days that you lie upon it you shall bear their iniquity.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb nasa' (H5375), "to bear," is the same term used in the Levitical scapegoat ritual (Lev 16:22) and in the Suffering Servant's vicarious bearing of iniquity (Isa 53:4,11). TYPOLOGY: Ezekiel's lying on his side as a penitential intercessory act prefigures the substitutionary bearing of sin, finding ultimate fulfillment in Christ who bore the iniquity of His people (Isa 53:6; 1 Pet 2:24). THEOLOGICAL: The prophet does not atone for the sin but embodies and dramatizes YHWH's judicial accounting of it.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: For I have assigned to you the years of their iniquity according to the number of the days — three hundred and ninety days — and you shall bear the iniquity of the house of Israel.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The figure of 390 days has generated extensive interpretive debate. One view equates 390 years with the period from the establishment of the northern kingdom's apostate cult under Jeroboam I (c. 930 BC) to the fall of Jerusalem (586 BC). Another reads it in light of the 70-year exile and Jubilee cycles. The LXX reads 190 days for Israel, suggesting textual variation existed early. THEOLOGICAL: The precision of the number underscores YHWH's meticulous covenantal accounting — the years of rebellion are numbered and not forgotten.

verses.flow: And when you have completed these, then lie down again upon your right side, and bear the iniquity of the house of Judah — forty days; a day for a year, a day for a year, I have assigned it to you.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "a day for a year" (yom lashanah, yom lashanah) is the hermeneutical key for the day-year principle employed also in Num 14:34 in the wilderness wandering narrative. THEOLOGICAL: This ratio establishes a pattern within prophetic literature used in subsequent apocalyptic calculation (Dan 9:24-27), and was applied by Second Temple interpreters to reckon the timing of messianic events.

verses.flow: And you, take for yourself wheat and barley and beans and lentils and millet and spelt, and put them in one vessel, and make them into bread for yourself; the number of days that you lie upon your side — three hundred and ninety days — you shall eat it.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The multi-grain mixture described here — wheat, barley, beans, lentils, millet, and spelt (kussemet, H3698) — represents the combining of coarse and diverse ingredients, signifying scarcity and the collapse of normal food supply. In ANE antiquity, mixing grain types was typically a mark of poverty or famine conditions. THEOLOGICAL: The siege ration bread is a sacramental inversion — instead of the whole-wheat show bread of YHWH's presence in the tabernacle, the exiles receive a coarse famine loaf, symbolizing covenantal curse (Deut 28:53-57).

verses.flow: And barley cake you shall eat it; and with human dung you shall bake it, before their eyes.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The command to bake on human dung constitutes the most extreme ritual defilement possible under the Levitical system (Deut 23:12-14), where human excrement was to be buried outside the camp precisely because YHWH walks in the midst of His people. The reversal of this command enacts symbolically the withdrawal of YHWH's holy presence from Jerusalem — the camp is no longer holy because YHWH has departed. TYPOLOGY: The desecration of the bread anticipates the Ezekiel 10 departure of the Glory (kavod).

verses.flow: And YHWH said: "Thus shall the sons of Israel eat their bread unclean, among the nations where I will drive them."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The eating of unclean bread "among the nations" directly invokes the covenant curse of Deut 28:64-68 and fulfills what Moses warned would come upon a disobedient Israel. HISTORICAL: Dietary law observance in exile was a pressing practical concern — Daniel 1 and later Second Temple literature (Tobit, Judith, 1 Maccabees) all reflect the gravity of maintaining purity while under Gentile dominion.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Then I said: "Ah, Lord YHWH! Behold, my soul has not been made unclean, and from my youth even until now I have not eaten what died of itself or was torn by beasts, nor has carrion flesh come into my mouth."
verses.note: HEBREW: The exclamation "Ah" translates 'ahah (H162), a cry of lamentation or protest used elsewhere by the prophets when confronting a divine decree perceived as overwhelming (Jer 1:6; 4:10). THEOLOGICAL: Ezekiel's protest on grounds of lifelong ritual purity is remarkable — he is a priest (kohen, 1:3), and his concern for defilement is not merely personal scruple but priestly vocation. The priest of YHWH must be ritually pure to stand before YHWH; this command threatens the very basis of his prophetic-priestly standing.

verses.flow: And he said to me: "Son of man, behold, I am breaking the staff of bread in Jerusalem, and they shall eat bread by weight and with anxiety, and water by measure and with horror they shall drink,
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "staff of bread" (matteh lechem, H4294 + H3899) is a striking idiom also found in Lev 26:26, Ps 105:16, and Ezek 5:16; 14:13. The matteh (staff/rod) serves as a metonym for the support or sustaining power of food — breaking it signals the complete withdrawal of YHWH's providential care from the covenantal community. THEOLOGICAL: This is an explicit covenant curse enacted in judgment (Lev 26:26), confirming that the siege of Jerusalem is not merely geopolitical but a direct expression of YHWH's covenantal discipline against a persistently rebellious people.

verses.flow: And you, son of man, take for yourself a sharp sword — a barber's razor you shall take for yourself — and you shall cause it to pass over your head and over your beard; then you shall take for yourself scales of balance and divide them.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word rendered "barber's razor" is ta'ar haggalabim (תַּעַר הַגַּלָּבִים; cf. Strong's H8593, H1532), literally "razor of the barbers." The act of shaving the head and beard was a sign of profound mourning and disgrace in the ancient Near East (cf. Isa 7:20; Job 1:20). Ezekiel's own body becomes the medium of prophetic sign-act (mofet), a recurring feature of his ministry. The sword used as a razor emphasizes that the instrument of Jerusalem's judgment is the sword of conquest.

verses.flow: Thus says Adonai YHWH: This is Jerusalem — I have set her in the midst of the nations, and countries are all around her.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The declaration that YHWH has placed Jerusalem "in the midst of the nations" reflects the Deuteronomy 32 / Psalm 82 worldview in which Israel occupies a privileged central position in YHWH's cosmic geography, the inheritance He reserved for Himself (Deut 32:8–9). Jerusalem's election was not national favoritism but covenantal responsibility — to be a light and witness among the nations. Her failure thus carries a uniquely amplified guilt.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: But she has rebelled against My ordinances (mishpatim) in wickedness more than the nations, and against My statutes more than the countries that are all around her; for they have rejected My ordinances and My statutes — they have not walked in them.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word mishpatim (מִשְׁפָּטִים; Strong's H4941) denotes divine "ordinances" or "judgments" — the binding legal decisions of YHWH as sovereign king. The parallel term chuqqot (חֻקּוֹת; Strong's H2708) denotes "statutes," the fixed and inscribed laws. Israel's rebellion is thus a legal default before the divine court, not merely moral failure. The comparative "more than the nations" is rhetorically devastating: pagan peoples, lacking the Torah, are being implicitly indicted as more responsive than covenant Israel.

verses.flow: Therefore thus says Adonai YHWH: Because you have multiplied more than the nations that are all around you, and in My statutes you have not walked, and My ordinances you have not done, and according to the ordinances of the nations that are all around you you have not even done —

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: therefore thus says Adonai YHWH: Behold, I — even I — am against you, and I will execute judgments (mishpatim) in your midst before the eyes of the nations.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "I — even I — am against you" (hineni elayich gam ani; הִנְנִי אֵלַיִךְ גַּם אֲנִי) is a declaration of divine war, using the form of a formal legal accusation before a sovereign assembly. The judgment is explicitly performed "before the eyes of the nations," indicating the cosmic and public dimension of YHWH's covenantal lawsuit. This echoes the divine council scene in which YHWH acts as supreme judge over all earthly history (Ps 82:1; Deut 32:36).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: And I will do in you that which I have not done, and the like of which I will not do again, because of all your abominations (to'evot).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "that which I have not done, and the like of which I will not do again" parallels the language of eschatological uniqueness found in Daniel 12:1 and echoed by Jesus in the Olivet Discourse (Matt 24:21; Mark 13:19). This hyperbolic intensification signals that the fall of Jerusalem is a typological event — a pattern pointing forward to both the 70 AD destruction and the final judgment. The word to'evot (תּוֹעֵבוֹת; Strong's H8441) denotes ritual and moral abominations, especially idolatry.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Therefore fathers shall eat sons in your midst, and sons shall eat their fathers; and I will execute judgments (mishpatim) against you, and all your remnant I will scatter to every wind.

Therefore, as I live — declares Adonai YHWH — surely because My sanctuary you have defiled with all your detestable things and with all your abominations (to'evot), even I will withdraw My eye, and I will not spare, and I also will have no pity.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "I will withdraw My eye" (gam ani lo' tachol eini) reflects YHWH's removal of His protective presence (shekhinah) from the sanctuary — a theme that reaches its climax in Ezekiel 10–11 with the departure of the Glory. This moment anticipates the cross, where the Son cries "My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me?" (Matt 27:46; Ps 22:1), experiencing the withdrawal of divine presence as the ultimate covenant judgment borne vicariously.

verses.flow: And My anger (af) shall be spent, and I will cause My wrath (hamati) to rest upon them, and I will be consoled; and they shall know that I, YHWH, have spoken in My zeal (kin'ati), when I have spent My fury (chamati) upon them.
verses.note: HEBREW: This verse contains three near-synonymous terms for divine wrath: af (אַף; Strong's H639), literally "nose/nostril," a vivid anthropomorphism for the flaring of wrath in the face; chamah (חֵמָה; Strong's H2534), "heat" or "burning anger"; and kin'ah (קִנְאָה; Strong's H7068), "zeal" or "jealousy," the passionate exclusivity of the covenant God who will not share His glory with another (Exod 20:5). The accumulation of these terms signals the extreme theological gravity of Israel's apostasy in violating the first and second commandments.

verses.flow: when I send against them the evil arrows of famine, which shall be for destruction, which I will send to destroy you; and famine I will increase upon you, and I will break your staff of bread.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "staff of bread" (matteh lechem; מַטֵּה לֶחֶם; cf. Lev 26:26) is a covenant-curse expression denoting the shattering of the food supply. The image of a staff being broken evokes the total collapse of sustenance. Archaeologically, sieges of ancient cities routinely weaponized starvation; Josephus (Jewish War V–VI) records the horrific famine conditions during the Roman siege of Jerusalem in 70 AD, which fulfilled this typological pattern in graphic historical detail.

verses.flow: And I will send upon you famine and evil beasts, and they shall bereave you; and pestilence and blood shall pass through you, and a sword I will bring upon you. I, YHWH, have spoken.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The fourfold judgment — famine, evil beasts, pestilence/blood, sword — echoes the covenant curses of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28, and prefigures the four horsemen of the Apocalypse (Rev 6:1–8). The repeated prophetic seal "I, YHWH, have spoken" (ani YHWH dibbarti) functions as the binding oath of the divine sovereign, guaranteeing the certainty of fulfillment. These judgments are not arbitrary punishments but the lawful execution of the Sinai covenant's own stipulated consequences.

verses.flow: and say: O mountains of Israel, hear the word of the Lord YHWH! Thus says the Lord YHWH to the mountains and to the hills, to the ravines and to the valleys: Behold, I — even I — am bringing a sword upon you, and I will destroy your high places (bamot).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Bamot" (H1116, from an unused root meaning "high place") refers to elevated outdoor shrines used for illicit worship throughout Canaan and Israel's history. The direct address to the mountains personifies the land itself as complicit in idolatry. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's judgment falls on the physical geography because sacred geography had been perverted — the high places that should have been avoided (Lev 26:30) became centers of Baal and Asherah worship. The land itself must be cleansed.

verses.flow: And your altars shall be laid waste and your incense altars (hammanim) shall be broken, and I will cast down your slain before your idols (gillulim).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hammanim" (H2553, likely related to "chamah," sun or heat) are incense altars or sun-pillars, cult objects associated with solar deities. "Gillulim" (H1544) is Ezekiel's characteristic term for idols, possibly derived from the word for dung-pellets (gelel), a deliberately contemptuous designation used 39 times in Ezekiel alone. THEOLOGICAL: The desecration of the altar by corpses is the ultimate defilement — YHWH turns the instruments of false worship into sites of judgment.

verses.flow: Then those of you who escape will remember me among the nations where they are carried captive — how I have been broken over their whoring heart that has turned away from me, and over their eyes that whore after their idols. And they shall loathe themselves for the evils they have committed, for all their abominations (toevot).
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "nishbarti" (niphal of shabar, H7665) rendered "I have been broken" is striking — YHWH describes himself as grieved or shattered by Israel's spiritual adultery. This is covenant-love language; the whoring (zanah, H2181) of the heart reflects covenantal infidelity rooted in desire. THEOLOGICAL: Divine pathos is real in Ezekiel's theology. YHWH is not an impassive deity but one whose covenant love (hesed) is wounded by unfaithfulness, anticipating the NT picture of God's grief over sin (Eph 4:30).

verses.flow: And you shall know that I am YHWH, when their slain lie among their idols, around their altars, on every high hill, on all the mountaintops, and under every green tree, and under every leafy oak — the places where they offered pleasing aroma (reyach nichoach) to all their idols.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Reyach nichoach" (H7381 + H5207, "soothing aroma" or "pleasing smell") is the sacrificial terminology from the Torah (Gen 8:21; Lev 1:9), here used ironically — the very language of legitimate YHWH-worship is applied to idolatrous sacrifice under trees and on hills. HISTORICAL: Sacred trees (asherim) and hilltop shrines were characteristic of Canaanite fertility religion. The "green tree" and "leafy oak" are standing idioms for illicit high-place worship (cf. Deut 12:2; 1 Kgs 14:23; Jer 2:20). TYPOLOGY: The irony anticipates NT inversion — the "aroma" of true worship is ultimately Christ's self-offering (Eph 5:2).

verses.flow: And I will stretch out my hand against them and make the land desolate and waste, more desolate than the wilderness of Diblah, throughout all their dwelling places. Then they shall know that I am YHWH."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Diblah" (or Riblah in some manuscripts — the LXX and several Hebrew MSS read "Riblah") was a city in the region of Hamath in Syria, representing the northern extreme of the land. Riblah was where Nebuchadnezzar executed the sons of Zedekiah (2 Kgs 25:6-7, 20-21). The wilderness of Diblah/Riblah thus evokes the full desolation of the land from south to north. THEOLOGICAL: The closing refrain "they shall know that I am YHWH" (yada, H3045) is Ezekiel's signature purpose-clause, appearing over 70 times in the book — even judgment is revelatory, serving YHWH's ultimate goal of being known.

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying:
"And you, son of man, thus says the Lord YHWH to the land of Israel: An end! The end has come upon the four corners of the land.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "four corners of the land" (arba kanfot ha'aretz) echoes the cosmic totality of creation (cf. Isa 11:12). The word "end" (ketz, H7093) is the same used in Gen 6:13 for the flood judgment — a deliberate intertextual echo suggesting that this judgment parallels the primordial undoing of creation. THEOLOGICAL: The repetition of "the end" in vv. 2-6 (occurring six times) functions as a rhetorical hammer, driving home the finality and absolute nature of the coming judgment. The covenant curses of Lev 26 and Deut 28 are reaching their culminating expression.

verses.flow: Now the end is upon you, and I will send my anger upon you, and I will judge you according to your ways, and I will bring upon you all your abominations (to'evot).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Abominations" (to'evot, H8441) is one of Ezekiel's most characteristic terms, appearing over 40 times in the book. It denotes ritual and moral acts that are fundamentally repugnant to YHWH's holy character. "Judge you according to your ways" establishes the lex talionis principle at the heart of covenantal judgment — the punishment mirrors and reflects the sin.

verses.flow: And my eye will not spare you, neither will I have pity; for I will bring upon you your ways, and your abominations shall be in your midst, and you shall know that I am YHWH.
Thus says the Lord YHWH: A disaster — a unique disaster — behold, it comes!
An end has come; the end has come! It has awakened against you. Behold, it comes!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "it has awakened against you" (he'ir, from 'ur, H5782) personifies the end as a sleeping adversary now roused. This language recalls divine warrior imagery common to the prophets. The staccato, drum-beat repetition ("an end, the end has come, the end has come") reflects a kind of prophetic ecstasy in the Hebrew, suggesting urgency beyond ordinary speech.

verses.flow: Behold, the day! Behold, it comes! The doom has gone out; the rod has budded, arrogance has blossomed.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "The rod has budded" (ha'matteh parach) echoes the blossoming rod of Aaron (Num 17:8), but inverted — where Aaron's rod budded as a sign of divine election and life, here the rod that buds is a rod of judgment and wickedness. THEOLOGICAL: "Arrogance has blossomed" (hazzadon parach) identifies pride as the root sin beneath all the covenant violations — a consistent biblical anthropology wherein hubris is the foundational transgression (cf. Prov 16:18; Isa 14:12-15).

verses.flow: Violence has risen up into a rod of wickedness. Nothing of them shall remain — none of their multitude, none of their wealth, and no pre-eminence among them.

verses.flow: The time has come; the day has arrived. Let not the buyer rejoice, nor the seller mourn, for wrath is upon all their multitude.

For the seller shall not return to what he has sold, even though they are yet alive; for the vision concerns the whole multitude — it shall not turn back, and a man shall not strengthen himself in the iniquity of his life.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Jubilee law (Lev 25) guaranteed the reversion of sold land to its original owner, offering perpetual hope of restoration. Ezekiel here pronounces that the coming judgment so utterly overturns the social order that even the Jubilee provisions become moot — no one will live to reclaim what they sold. This is among the most radical inversions of Torah promise in prophetic literature.

verses.flow: They have blown the trumpet and made everything ready, but no one goes to battle, for my wrath is upon all their multitude.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The blowing of the trumpet (taqoa ba'tsotseret) was the standard military summons for battle assembly (Num 10:9; Joel 2:1). The grim irony is that the full military apparatus is activated, yet no one responds — not from cowardice alone, but because YHWH's wrath has rendered human resistance futile. Divine sovereignty has already determined the outcome.

verses.flow: Their silver they shall cast out into the streets, and their gold shall become an abhorrent thing (niddah); their silver and their gold shall not be able to deliver them in the day of the wrath of YHWH. They shall not satisfy their appetite, and they shall not fill their stomachs, because it has been the stumbling block of their iniquity.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Abhorrent thing" (niddah, H5079) is the term used for menstrual impurity in the Levitical code — applying it to silver and gold is a startling desacralization of wealth. In the ancient Near East, precious metals carried sacral connotations as gifts of the gods and symbols of divine blessing. Ezekiel deliberately inverts this, declaring that coveted wealth becomes ceremonially unclean. THEOLOGICAL: The stumbling block (mikhshol, H4383) of iniquity points to the idolatrous use of silver and gold in crafting images (v. 20), indicating that material prosperity, when divorced from covenant loyalty, becomes the very mechanism of spiritual ruin.

Some manuscripts and commentators read this as "my hidden treasure" (i.e., the Holy of Holies). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The defilement of the Temple is not merely a political catastrophe but a cosmic one — YHWH's earthly throne-room is violated, mirroring the rebellion in the heavenly council (cf. Ps 82; Ezek 28). The turning of YHWH's face (v. 22) is the formal withdrawal of divine presence, the reversal of the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:25-26).

verses.flow: Disaster upon disaster will come, and rumor upon rumor; they shall seek a vision from a prophet, but the Torah shall perish from the priest, and counsel from the elders.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The threefold failure — no vision from the prophet, no Torah from the priest, no counsel from the elder — represents the total collapse of Israel's mediatorial infrastructure. These three offices corresponded to the three primary channels of divine communication in Israel. TYPOLOGY: This total communicative silence anticipates the "famine of hearing the words of YHWH" (Amos 8:11-12) and finds its ultimate resolution in Christ, who is prophet, priest, and king — the one mediator in whom all channels of divine communication are embodied and perfected (Heb 1:1-2).

verses.flow: The king shall mourn, and the prince shall be clothed in horror, and the hands of the people of the land shall be paralyzed with terror. According to their way I will do to them, and according to their judgments I will judge them, and they shall know that I am YHWH."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing formula "they shall know that I am YHWH" (yada'tem ki ani YHWH) is the definitive refrain of Ezekiel, occurring approximately 70 times in the book. Even in judgment, YHWH's purpose is self-disclosure and the restoration of covenant knowledge. TYPOLOGY: The triad of "king… prince… people of the land" captures the total social hierarchy experiencing collapse — from apex to base. This total societal dissolution sets the stage for the new covenant promises of chapters 36-37 and the eschatological restoration of chapters 40-48, where a new Prince (nasi) reigns under YHWH's direct kingship.

verses.flow: And it came to pass in the sixth year, in the sixth month, on the fifth day of the month, as I sat in my house, and the elders of Judah were sitting before me, that the hand of the Lord YHWH fell upon me there.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The date formula places this vision in September 592 BC, exactly fourteen months after Ezekiel's inaugural vision (1:1-2). The "elders of Judah" sitting before Ezekiel suggests he functioned as an oracular consultant among the exiles in Tel-abib. The "hand of YHWH" (yad YHWH) is a recurring formula in Ezekiel (1:3; 3:14, 22) indicating prophetic compulsion or ecstatic transport, paralleling similar language for Elijah (1 Kgs 18:46).

verses.flow: And I looked, and behold, a likeness as the appearance of fire — from the appearance of his loins and downward, fire; and from his loins and upward, as the appearance of brightness, as the color of gleaming metal (hashmal).
verses.note: HEBREW: The figure described closely mirrors the divine being of Ezekiel 1:26-27. "Hashmal" (Strong's H2830) is a hapax-rich term of debated meaning — possibly electrum (a gold-silver alloy) or a supernatural luminescent substance; the LXX renders it "elektron." The description of fire from the loins downward and radiance upward may distinguish the divine attendant from YHWH himself, or it may be a theophanic manifestation of YHWH's own glory.

verses.flow: And he put forth the form of a hand and took me by a lock of my head, and the ruach (ruach) lifted me up between earth and heaven, and brought me in visions of God to Jerusalem, to the entrance of the inner gate that faces north, where was the seat of the image of jealousy, which provokes to jealousy.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "image of jealousy" (semel haqqin'ah) placed at the gate provocatively asserts a rival deity's claim at the very threshold of YHWH's house. The word "semel" (Strong's H5566) appears only here and in Deut 4:16, likely referring to an Asherah-pole or a carved cultic statue. The location "that provokes to jealousy" (hammaqneh) reflects the covenantal language of YHWH as a jealous God (El qanna', Exod 20:5), framing idolatry as covenant adultery. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The transport "between earth and heaven" echoes ancient cosmological language of the prophet crossing into the divine realm to receive council intelligence.

verses.flow: And behold, the glory (kavod) of the God of Israel was there, like the vision that I had seen in the valley.

verses.flow: And he said to me, "Son of man, do you see what they are doing — the great abominations (to'evot) that the house of Israel is committing here, to drive me far from my sanctuary? Yet you will see still greater abominations."
verses.note: HEBREW: "To'evot" (Strong's H8441, abominations) is a cultic-legal term for ritually and morally offensive acts, particularly idolatry. YHWH's complaint that Israel's sin drives him "far from my sanctuary" (miqdash) is theologically stunning — it is the people's unfaithfulness, not divine abandonment, that sets in motion the departure of the kavod (glory) documented progressively through chapters 9-11. TYPOLOGY: This indictment pattern foreshadows the NT theme of defiling the temple of God's Spirit (1 Cor 3:16-17; 6:19).

verses.flow: And he said to me, "Go in and see the evil abominations that they are committing here."

verses.flow: So I went in and looked, and behold, every form of creeping things and detestable beasts, and all the idols (gillulim) of the house of Israel, were engraved on the wall all around.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The menagerie of "creeping things and detestable beasts" (remes uv'hemah shaqets) engraved on the walls strongly evokes Egyptian cultic iconography, where animal-headed deities were commonly depicted in temple reliefs. The syncretism here likely reflects the influence of Egypt-aligned political factions in Jerusalem (cf. Ezek 17), who had adopted corresponding religious practices. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "gillulim" (idols, Strong's H1544 — a derogatory term literally meaning "dung-pellets") represent the worship of entities that, in the divine council worldview, are lesser spiritual beings unlawfully receiving the worship due to YHWH alone (Deut 32:17; Ps 106:37).

verses.flow: And seventy men of the elders of the house of Israel, with Jaazaniah the son of Shaphan standing among them, were standing before them, each with his censer in his hand, and the fragrance of the cloud of incense rising.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The number seventy is significant — it mirrors the seventy elders of Israel who ate and drank in the presence of God on Sinai (Exod 24:9-11) and the seventy members of the Sanhedrin-like council. Here, they are subverted into a secret idolatrous assembly. Jaazaniah son of Shaphan is striking because Shaphan's family were reforming scribal loyalists to YHWH (2 Kgs 22:8-14), making this son's participation a pointed act of apostasy from his household's legacy.

verses.flow: And he said to me, "Son of man, have you seen what the elders of the house of Israel are doing in the dark, each man in his chambers of images? For they say, 'YHWH does not see us, YHWH has forsaken the land.'"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The theological error stated here — "YHWH does not see us, YHWH has forsaken the land" — is a form of practical atheism masquerading as theological despair. It inverts the truth: it is Israel who has forsaken YHWH, not YHWH who has forsaken Israel. The secret chamber rituals (cheder maskiyyot, "chambers of his image-room") suggest mystery-cult initiatory practices, paralleling ANE guild-religion where hidden rites were accessible only to the initiated.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And he said to me, "You will see still greater abominations that they are committing."

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And he brought me to the entrance of the gate of the house of YHWH that is toward the north, and behold, there were women sitting and weeping for Tammuz.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Tammuz (Sumerian: Dumuzi) was a Mesopotamian dying-and-rising god, the consort of Ishtar/Inanna, mourned annually in a summer ritual of lamentation corresponding to the seasonal withering of vegetation. His cult was widespread across the ANE. The presence of women weeping for Tammuz in the Jerusalem temple complex represents a direct importation of Babylonian/Mesopotamian fertility religion into the sacred precincts of YHWH. This is the only explicit mention of Tammuz in the Hebrew Bible. DIVINE_COUNCIL: From a divine council perspective, Tammuz represents a member of the lesser divine assembly whose worship constituted a direct usurpation of YHWH's exclusive rights over Israel.

verses.flow: And he said to me, "Have you seen this, O son of man? You will see still greater abominations than these."

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And he brought me into the inner court of the house of YHWH, and behold, at the entrance of the temple of YHWH, between the porch and the altar, were about twenty-five men, with their backs toward the temple of YHWH and their faces toward the east, and they were bowing down eastward to the sun.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The twenty-five men with backs turned toward the temple and faces toward the rising sun represents the most shocking abomination of all — temple personnel (likely priests) performing solar worship within the inner court itself. Solar worship was associated with Shamash in Mesopotamia and with various Egyptian solar cults. The act of turning their backs to the Holy of Holies — the dwelling place of the kavod — is a deliberate liturgical reversal, a posture of contempt toward YHWH. TYPOLOGY: This image of religious leaders corrupting the innermost sanctuary prefigures Christ's cleansing of the temple (Matt 21:12-13; John 2:13-17) and Paul's warning of the "man of lawlessness" who sits in the temple of God (2 Thess 2:4).

verses.flow: And he said to me, "Have you seen this, O son of man? Is it too light a thing for the house of Judah to commit the abominations that they commit here, that they fill the land with violence and provoke me to anger still further? And behold, they put the branch to their nose.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "they put the branch to their nose" (shalchu et-hazemorah el-appam) is famously obscure. Some scholars suggest it refers to a Zoroastrian or Mesopotamian ritual involving a sacred bundle held near the face during worship. The MT reads "their nose" (appam) while some manuscripts and the Masoretic marginal reading (qere) substitute "my nose" (appi) as a scribal euphemism to avoid an irreverent image of the worshipers' gesture reaching toward YHWH himself. THEOLOGICAL: The accumulation of four distinct abominations in this chapter (the image of jealousy, the secret animal-idol cult, the Tammuz mourning rites, and solar worship) represents a comprehensive survey of syncretistic corruption spanning Canaanite, Mesopotamian, Egyptian, and astral religion — Israel has imported the entire pagan world into YHWH's house.

verses.flow: Therefore I also will act in wrath (chemah). My eye will not spare, nor will I have pity. And though they cry in my ears with a loud voice, I will not hear them."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The divine declaration of wrath (chemah, Strong's H2534 — burning anger, fury) and the withdrawal of pity frames the coming judgment of chapters 9-11 as just and inevitable. The phrase "my eye will not spare" (lo-tachus 'eini) becomes a refrain throughout chapters 9-11, underscoring that the destruction of Jerusalem is not divine failure but divine holiness. TYPOLOGY: The un-hearing of their loud cries parallels Proverbs 1:28 and ultimately prefigures the eschatological motif of those who called "Lord, Lord" being turned away (Matt 7:22-23), emphasizing that persistent covenant rejection exhausts divine forbearance.

verses.flow: And He called in my ears with a loud voice, saying, "Draw near, O executioners of the city, each with his destroying weapon in his hand."
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb translated "draw near" (qarev) implies a summons into proximity for a solemn purpose. The "executioners" (pquddot ha-ir) literally means "appointed ones of the city" or "those charged over the city" — a term that carries overtones of delegated divine authority. DIVINE_COUNCIL: These six figures, appearing in response to a heavenly summons, reflect the divine council motif in which heavenly beings (likely angelic enforcers) are dispatched to carry out divine judgment; compare (Ps 82:1), (Zech 1:8-11), and the "sons of God" executing YHWH's decrees.

verses.flow: And behold, six men were coming from the direction of the upper gate, which faces north, each with his shattering weapon in his hand; and one man among them was clothed in linen, with a writing case (qeset) of a scribe at his side. And they came in and stood beside the bronze altar.
verses.note: HEBREW: The "writing case" (qeset, Strong's H7083) is an Egyptian loanword (gstj) referring to a scribal palette or ink-case, used for writing instruments. Its presence on the linen-clad figure marks him as distinct — a recording and marking figure rather than a destroying one. TYPOLOGY: The linen garment consistently distinguishes priestly or heavenly figures in Ezekiel (cf. Ezek 10:2, Dan 10:5, 12:6-7), foreshadowing the heavenly high priest of (Heb 4:14), and the angel of (Rev 1:13) who is identified with the glorified Christ.

verses.flow: Now the glory (kavod) of the God of Israel had gone up from upon the cherub, on which it had been, to the threshold of the house. And He called to the man clothed in linen, who had the writing case of the scribe at his side.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The departure of the kavod (glory, H3519) of YHWH from the cherubim to the threshold marks a catastrophic transition — the divine presence is beginning its withdrawal from the defiled temple. This is the first stage of the glory's departure that will culminate in Ezek 10-11. TYPOLOGY: The departure of the Shekinah presence anticipates the "departure" of Christ from the temple (Matt 23:38-39, Matt 24:1), after which judgment follows.

verses.flow: And YHWH said to him, "Pass through the midst of the city, through the midst of Jerusalem, and put a mark (tav) on the foreheads of the men who groan and who cry out over all the abominations that are done in its midst."
verses.note: HEBREW: The "mark" placed on foreheads is the Hebrew letter tav (H8420), which in ancient Paleo-Hebrew script was written as an X or + shape — a cross-like form. This has attracted significant theological attention in early Jewish and Christian interpretation (Origen, Tertullian). The verb "groan" (anach, H584) and "cry out" (naaq, H602) depict an interior anguish over corporate sin, not merely personal wrongdoing. TYPOLOGY: The sealing of the faithful on their foreheads before divine judgment is a recurring motif that reaches its fullest expression in (Rev 7:3, 9:4, 14:1), where the servants of God are sealed by the Lamb — a direct structural parallel to this passage. The tav-mark distinguishes the remnant who mourn over sin from those who embrace it.

verses.flow: Old man, young man, and virgin, and little children and women — you shall slay to destruction; but do not come near any man on whom is the mark. And begin from My sanctuary." So they began from the elders who were before the house.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The command to begin judgment "from My sanctuary" (miqqodshi) is theologically sobering — the elders standing before the house bear the greatest culpability, echoing the principle that greater proximity to divine revelation entails greater accountability (cf. Amos 3:2, Luke 12:48). The universal scope (old, young, virgin, child, woman) underscores that covenant violation brings comprehensive judgment. TYPOLOGY: This pattern — judgment beginning at the house of God — is explicitly applied to the NT community in (1 Pet 4:17), signaling a typological continuity in divine governance.

verses.flow: And it came to pass, while they were striking, and I alone remained, that I fell upon my face and cried out and said, "Ah, Lord YHWH! Are You destroying all the remnant of Israel by pouring out Your wrath upon Jerusalem?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Ezekiel's intercessory cry ("Are You destroying all the remnant?") recalls the intercessions of Moses (Exod 32:11-14) and Amos (Amos 7:2-5). The title "Lord YHWH" (Adonai YHWH) is Ezekiel's characteristic address, occurring over 200 times in the book, emphasizing both the absolute sovereignty and personal covenantal relationship of God. HISTORICAL: The "remnant" (she'erit, H7611) theology is central to prophetic hope — even in Ezekiel's darkest visions, the question of remnant preservation drives the redemptive arc toward chapters 36-37.

verses.flow: And He said to me, "The iniquity of the house of Israel and Judah is exceedingly great, and the land is full of blood, and the city is full of perversity (matteh); for they say, 'YHWH has forsaken the land, and YHWH does not see.'
verses.note: HEBREW: "Perversity" (matteh, H4297) can also be rendered "violence" or "oppression" — a bending or twisting of justice. The declaration "YHWH does not see" (ein YHWH ro'eh) represents practical atheism: a functional denial of divine omniscience driving moral collapse. This mirrors the false theology refuted in (Ps 94:7-9). THEOLOGICAL: The pairing of "blood" (dam) and "perversity" recalls the creation of conditions that caused the Flood (Gen 6:11-13), casting Jerusalem's judgment in terms of a new cosmic reckoning against unchecked violence and injustice.

verses.flow: And I also — My eye shall not spare, and I will have no pity; I will bring their way upon their head."

verses.flow: And behold, the man clothed in linen, who had the writing case at his side, brought back word, saying, "I have done as You commanded me."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The linen-clad figure's report of completion ("I have done as You commanded me") closes the vision with a note of precise divine sovereignty — heavenly agents execute the divine will fully and without failure. TYPOLOGY: This completion report anticipates the language of the Son in (John 17:4, 19:30), where Christ declares His commission fulfilled. The structure of commission, execution, and completion-report is a motif in both the divine council and incarnational mission frameworks.

verses.flow: Then I looked, and behold, above the expanse that was over the heads of the cherubim there appeared above them something like a sapphire stone, in appearance like the likeness of a throne.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The sapphire throne-platform (Heb. sappir, H5601) above the cherubim recalls Exod 24:10, where Moses and the elders see the God of Israel standing on "a pavement of sapphire stone." This deliberate echo establishes Ezekiel's vision within the stream of authorized divine appearances, anchoring the prophet's legitimacy. The throne motif signals that YHWH remains sovereign even as His glory begins the dreadful departure from the defiled temple.

verses.flow: And he spoke to the man clothed in linen and said, "Go in between the whirling wheels, beneath the cherub, and fill your hands with coals of fire from between the cherubim, and scatter them over the city." And he went in before my eyes.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The command to scatter burning coals over Jerusalem evokes both the altar-coal purification of Isaiah (Isa 6:6-7) and the divine judgment fire of Sodom (Gen 19:24). Here the direction is reversed — fire goes from sacred space outward onto an unrepentant city. The "man clothed in linen" parallels the angelic intercessor-scribe of Ezek 9:2-3 and anticipates the priestly-intercessory figure of Christ (Rev 1:13), who alone stands in the council's presence.

verses.flow: Then the glory (kavod) of YHWH rose up from above the cherub to the threshold of the house, and the house was filled with the cloud, and the court was filled with the brightness of the glory of YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: Kavod (H3519, from kaved, "to be heavy/weighty") is the characteristic term for YHWH's visible, luminous presence. Its movement here — rising from above the cherub to the threshold — represents the first stage of the glory's departure from the inner sanctuary (cf. Ezek 9:3; 11:22-23). This staged withdrawal is theologically ominous: YHWH does not abandon His people in a moment but in measured steps, giving opportunity for repentance.

verses.flow: And the sound of the wings of the cherubim was heard as far as the outer court, like the voice of El Shaddai when he speaks.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "voice of El Shaddai" (qol Shaddai, H410 + H7706) is remarkably rare in the prophets and carries archaic, patriarchal resonance (Gen 17:1; Exod 6:3). The sound of cherubic wings being compared to the divine voice implies the cherubim are not merely furniture but active members of the heavenly court (divine council), whose movements are themselves a form of divine speech. Compare the "four living creatures" of Rev 4:6-8.

verses.flow: And the cherub stretched out his hand from between the cherubim to the fire that was between the cherubim, and took some of it and put it into the hands of the man clothed in linen, who took it and went out.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The cherub's act of mediating fire to the human agent illustrates the layered structure of divine-council governance: YHWH commands, the angelic beings execute, and human agents carry out on earth. The fire taken from "between the cherubim" is the same fire associated with the divine presence (Lev 16:12; cf. the ark's mercy seat, flanked by cherubim). Judgment originates in the holy of holies.

verses.flow: When they went, they went in any of their four directions without turning as they went, but in whatever direction the head faced they went, without turning as they went.
And their whole body, and their backs, and their hands, and their wings, and the wheels were full of eyes all around — the wheels of all four of them.
As for the wheels, they were called in my hearing "the whirling wheels" (galgal).
verses.note: HEBREW: Galgal (H1534) means "wheel" or "whirlwind" and shares its root with the noun used in Ps 77:18 and Isa 17:13 for a rolling, tumbling force. The specific naming of these wheels in Ezekiel's hearing emphasizes their numinous reality — they are not merely mechanical parts of a vision but recognized, named entities within the heavenly assembly. The Targum renders this with heightened angelic significance.

verses.flow: And each of the cherubim had four faces: the first face was the face of the cherub, and the second face was the face of a man, and the third the face of a lion, and the fourth the face of an eagle.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The substitution of the "face of a cherub" for the "face of an ox" (cf. Ezek 1:10) is unique to chapter 10 and has generated extensive discussion. The cherub face likely reflects the influence of the temple setting on the vision's imagery, as cherubim dominated the holy of holies (1 Kgs 6:23-28). Some scholars suggest Ezekiel or a later editor made the substitution to acknowledge that the "living creatures" of chapter 1 and the "cherubim" of chapter 10 are identical. The four faces together represent the totality of creaturely life under divine authority.

verses.flow: And the cherubim were lifted up — this is the living creature (chayah) that I saw by the river Chebar.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The river Chebar (Heb. nahar Kebar) was a major irrigation canal in Babylonia, likely the Naru Kabari known from cuneiform sources, near the city of Nippur. The exiles were settled in this region (Ezek 1:1; 3:15). The identification of the living creatures with those seen at Chebar is a crucial editorial note affirming the unity of the visions and the global reach of YHWH's sovereignty — He is not merely the God of Canaan but rules over the very empires that hold Israel captive.

verses.flow: When they stood still, these stood still, and when they were lifted up, these lifted themselves up with them, for the spirit (ruach) of the living creature was in them.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The ruach (H7307, "spirit/breath/wind") of the living creature inhabiting the wheels reflects the integrated, spirit-animated nature of the divine-council machinery. Unlike the inert mechanical wheels of human construction, these wheels share in the vitality of the cherubim. This parallels the cosmic-ocean imagery of ancient Near Eastern creation accounts where divine spirit animates chaotic matter; here it is applied to the ordered instruments of divine judgment and governance.

verses.flow: Then the glory (kavod) of YHWH went out from the threshold of the house and stood over the cherubim.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The departure of the kavod (glory) from the threshold to stand over the cherubim represents the second stage of the glory's withdrawal (cf. v. 4 for the first). The full departure will be completed in Ezek 11:22-23, when the glory moves east to the Mount of Olives — a detail of profound typological significance, as the same location will host the Messiah's eschatological return (Zech 14:4; Acts 1:11-12). The departure is not abandonment but relocation, anticipating ultimate restoration (Ezek 43:1-5).

verses.flow: And the cherubim lifted up their wings and rose up from the earth before my eyes when they went out, with the wheels beside them. And they stood at the entrance of the east gate of the house of YHWH, and the glory of the God of Israel was over them above.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The glory of YHWH departing through the east gate carries messianic weight. The east gate of the Second Temple was sealed shut according to rabbinic tradition and Ezekiel's own vision (Ezek 44:2), reserved for the prince who would enter through it. Christian interpreters have long identified this with Christ's triumphal entry (Matt 21:1-11) and eschatological return. The glory's exit through the east gate in judgment is thus the photographic negative of the glory's promised return through the same gate in redemption (Ezek 43:2-4).

verses.flow: These are the living creatures that I saw beneath the God of Israel by the river Chebar, and I knew that they were cherubim.
Each had four faces, and each had four wings, and underneath their wings was the likeness of human hands.
And as for the likeness of their faces — they were the faces whose appearance I had seen by the river Chebar, their appearance and they themselves. Each one went straight ahead.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The chapter closes with a deliberate identification formula tying Ezekiel's temple vision to the inaugural chariot-throne vision of chapter 1. This literary bracketing (inclusio) confirms that the departure of the divine kavod is carried out by the same heavenly beings first revealed at the prophet's call. The repetition of "each one went straight ahead" (cf. Ezek 1:9, 12) emphasizes the purposeful, unstoppable nature of YHWH's movements — whether in calling a prophet or in executing covenantal judgment.

verses.flow: And the ruach (spirit/wind) lifted me up and brought me to the eastern gate of the house of YHWH, which faces eastward. And behold, at the entrance of the gate there were twenty-five men, and I saw among them Jaazaniah the son of Azzur, and Pelatiah the son of Benaiah, princes of the people.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The eastern gate of the temple complex faces the Mount of Olives, the same direction from which the kavod departs (v. 23). The twenty-five men likely represent a ruling council of Jerusalem. Jaazaniah ben Azzur is distinct from the Jaazaniah ben Shaphan of Ezekiel 8:11, indicating multiple corrupt leaders. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The positioning at the eastern gate mirrors the directional axis of divine departure, suggesting these human leaders have usurped the position of divine council functionaries.

verses.flow: And he said to me, "Son of man, these are the men who devise iniquity and who give wicked counsel in this city,
who say, 'The time is not near to build houses; this city is the pot, and we are the meat.'
verses.note: HEBREW: The proverb "this city is the pot (sir) and we are the meat (basar)" reflects false confidence in Jerusalem's protective walls. The leaders believed the city's fortifications made them invulnerable, inverting the earlier prophetic warning. The term sir (Strong's H5518) elsewhere describes a cooking vessel; the irony is that they misread their own vulnerability as security. THEOLOGICAL: Their theology of divine inviolability — rooted in a misapplication of Zion theology — blinded them to imminent judgment.

verses.flow: Therefore prophesy against them; prophesy, O son of man."

verses.flow: And the ruach of YHWH fell upon me, and he said to me, "Say, thus says YHWH: So you say, O house of Israel; for I know the things that come into your mind.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "the ruach of YHWH fell upon me" (watippol alai ruach YHWH) uses the verb naphal (H5307), indicating a sudden, powerful descent of the divine spirit upon the prophet, distinct from a gentle anointing. This formulaic expression distinguishes Ezekiel's ecstatic prophetic states from mere visionary experience. THEOLOGICAL: The divine omniscience of inner thoughts ("I know the things that come into your mind") echoes Psalm 139 and anticipates Christ's discernment of hearts (John 2:24-25).
