verses.flow: You have multiplied your slain in this city, and you have filled its streets with the slain.
Therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: Your slain whom you have laid in the midst of it — they are the meat, and this city is the pot; but you shall be brought out from the midst of it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH subverts the leaders' own metaphor. They claimed to be the protected "meat" in the pot of Jerusalem, but YHWH declares the true "meat" is the innocent slain whom they murdered. The guilty will be cast out — precisely reversing their boast. This divine irony is a consistent pattern in prophetic judgment oracles.

verses.flow: You have feared the sword, and the sword I will bring upon you, declares the Lord YHWH.

verses.flow: And it came to pass, when I prophesied, that Pelatiah the son of Benaiah died. Then I fell down upon my face and cried with a loud voice and said, "Ah, Lord YHWH! Will you make a full end of the remnant of Israel?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Pelatiah's sudden death during the prophetic act demonstrates the performative power of prophetic speech — the word spoken in YHWH's name effects the reality it declares (cf. Isaiah 55:11). Ezekiel's intercession ("Will you make a full end of the remnant?") echoes Moses's pleading (Exodus 32:11-13) and establishes him as a mediatorial prophet. TYPOLOGY: The prophet's intercessory grief foreshadows Christ's weeping over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44).

verses.flow: Therefore say, Thus says the Lord YHWH: Though I removed them far off among the nations, and though I scattered them among the lands, yet I have been a sanctuary (miqdash) for them in small measure in the lands where they have gone.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term miqdash (H4720, sanctuary/holy place) is used here in a remarkable diminutive or qualified sense — "a sanctuary in small measure" (miqdash me'at). This is theologically significant: YHWH himself becomes the portable sanctuary for the exiles, replacing the Jerusalem temple. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is foundational for the development of synagogue theology and the understanding that divine presence is not bound to a physical structure, anticipating John 4:21-24 and the NT temple-in-believers motif.

verses.flow: And I will give them one heart, and a new ruach I will put within you; and I will remove the heart of stone from their flesh and give them a heart of flesh,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The promise of a "new ruach" and a "heart of flesh" replacing the "heart of stone" is one of the clearest anticipations of the New Covenant in the Hebrew Bible, paralleling Jeremiah 31:31-34. This promise finds its fulfillment at Pentecost with the outpouring of the Holy Spirit (Acts 2; 2 Corinthians 3:3). The "one heart" (lev echad) signals covenantal unity and undivided allegiance to YHWH. HEBREW: Lev (H3820) denotes the seat of will, intellect, and emotion; its transformation is total anthropological renewal.

Its placement after the promise of spiritual renewal (v. 19) confirms that true covenant relationship requires inner transformation, not merely external compliance with law.

verses.flow: Then the keruvim (cherubim) lifted up their wings, with the wheels beside them; and the kavod (glory) of the God of Israel was over them above.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The keruvim (cherubim, H3742) are the throne-bearers of YHWH's heavenly court, representing the highest order of divine council members who sustain the divine chariot (merkavah). Their synchronized movement with the wheels (ofanim) indicates the integrated divine war machine of the heavenly host. The kavod (H3519, glory/weightiness) departing signals YHWH's formal abandonment of the temple, a catastrophic theological event — the shekinah withdrawing its protection.

verses.flow: And the kavod of YHWH went up from the midst of the city and stood upon the mountain which is on the east side of the city.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The kavod of YHWH stopping on the Mount of Olives is profoundly significant. This is the same mountain from which Jesus ascended (Acts 1:11-12) and to which he will return (Zechariah 14:4). The divine glory pauses here — not departing entirely — as if in waiting. TYPOLOGY: The Mount of Olives thus becomes the hinge-point of both divine departure in judgment and divine return in redemption, bracketing the entire interadventual period.

verses.flow: And the ruach lifted me up and brought me in the vision by the ruach of God into Chaldea, to the exiles. Then the vision that I had seen went up from me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prophet is transported back to the exiles in Babylon by the ruach, confirming that the entire vision of chapters 8-11 was received in a visionary state while Ezekiel was physically among the exiles. This "vision within vision" structure (cf. Revelation 1:10; 4:2) underscores the apocalyptic literary genre of the book and the reality of prophetic transport experiences in Second Temple Judaism.

verses.flow: And I spoke to the exiles all the things that YHWH had shown me.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Ezekiel's report to the exiles serves as both prophetic warning and covenantal witness. The exiles in Babylon would receive this word approximately 593 BCE, roughly six years before Jerusalem's fall in 587/586 BCE. His proclamation authenticated by the vision of divine departure confirmed what false prophets denied: the temple's fall was certain, and YHWH himself had decreed it.

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying:
"Son of man, you are dwelling in the midst of a rebellious house (beit meri), who have eyes to see but do not see, who have ears to hear but do not hear, for they are a rebellious house.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Rebellious house" (beit meri) — meri derives from the root marah (H4805), meaning to be contentious, defiant, or bitter in rebellion. This is a recurring indictment formula in Ezekiel (cf. 2:5-8; 3:9, 26), characterizing Israel's fundamental covenantal failure. The pairing of unseeing eyes and unhearing ears echoes the Shema's call and its inversion, anticipating Isaiah 6:9-10 and Jesus's citation of that passage (Matt 13:14-15).

verses.flow: Therefore, son of man, prepare for yourself an exile's baggage (keli golah), and go into exile by day before their eyes; and you shall go into exile from your place to another place before their eyes — perhaps they will see, though they are a rebellious house.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The enacted sign of the exile's baggage (keli golah) functions as a prophetic mime-act — a form of symbolic prophecy common in the ancient Near East and uniquely developed in Ezekiel (cf. chs. 4-5). The prophet's body becomes the living text of the message. This typology of the prophet embodying the fate of the people anticipates the NT understanding of Christ bearing the judgment of exile on behalf of his people (Gal 3:13).

verses.flow: And you shall bring out your baggage as baggage for exile by day, before their eyes; and you yourself shall go out in the evening before their eyes, as those who go into exile.

verses.flow: Before their eyes you shall lift it upon your shoulder, and carry it out in the dark; you shall cover your face so that you cannot see the land — for I have set you as a sign (mofet) for the house of Israel."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Sign" (mofet, H4159) — from an uncertain root, possibly related to pala' (to be wondrous); denotes a miraculous or portentous sign, distinguishable from 'ot (token-sign). The covering of the face (covering so as not to see the land) prefigures the blinding of Zedekiah (2 Kgs 25:7; Jer 52:11), a detail that validates the predictive precision of Ezekiel's enacted prophecy.

verses.flow: And I will spread my net (resheti) over him, and he shall be caught in my snare (mikhmori); and I will bring him to Babylon, the land of the Chaldeans — yet he shall not see it, and there he shall die.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "net" (reshet) and "snare" (mikhmor) are divine-judgment metaphors drawn from the imagery of the hunter and fowler (cf. Ps 91:3; Hos 7:12). The prophecy that the prince (Zedekiah) would be brought to Babylon "yet shall not see it" was fulfilled with remarkable precision — Nebuchadnezzar put out Zedekiah's eyes at Riblah before taking him to Babylon (2 Kgs 25:7), so he arrived there blind. This fulfillment stands as a key validation of Ezekiel's prophetic authority.

verses.flow: And all who are around him, his helpers and all his troops, I will scatter to every wind, and I will unsheathe the sword after them.

verses.flow: But I will allow a few men of them to escape the sword, from famine, and from plague, so that they may recount all their abominations among the nations to which they go; and they shall know that I am YHWH."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The preservation of a remnant to "recount all their abominations" among the nations serves a missional purpose within judgment — the scattered few become witnesses to YHWH's holiness and justice among the Gentiles. This covenantal remnant theology anticipates the Isaianic servant passages and the NT concept of the church as a diaspora people bearing testimony (1 Pet 1:1; 2:9-12).

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying:
"Son of man, eat your bread with quaking (ra'ash), and drink your water with trembling (re'adah) and with anxiety.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Quaking" (ra'ash, H7494) refers to a violent shaking or trembling, used elsewhere for earthquake (Amos 1:1) and divine theophany (Ezek 3:12). "Trembling" (re'adah, H7461) denotes a shivering anxiety; it occurs rarely in the OT. The two terms together create a picture of total physical and psychological dissolution — a somatic enactment of the terror that the siege of Jerusalem would produce.

verses.flow: And the inhabited cities shall be laid waste, and the land shall become a desolation; and you shall know that I am YHWH."

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying:

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: "Son of man, what is this proverb (mashal) you have in the land of Israel, saying: 'The days grow long and every vision (chazon) perishes'?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Proverb" (mashal, H4912) — a pithy saying or byword used to encapsulate a recognized truth or popular attitude. The cynical proverb ("the days grow long and every vision perishes") represents a form of practical skepticism toward prophetic eschatology — a delay in fulfillment interpreted as disproof. THEOLOGICAL: This proverb reflects the danger identified also in 2 Peter 3:3-4, where scoffers use the delay of divine judgment as an argument against its reality. YHWH's response is not an extension of the timeline but an acceleration.

verses.flow: Therefore say to them: Thus says the Lord YHWH: I will put an end to this proverb, and they shall no more use it as a proverb in Israel; but say to them: The days are near, and the fulfillment (dabar) of every vision.
For there shall be no more any false vision (chazon shav) or flattering divination (mikesem chalaq) within the house of Israel.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "False vision" (chazon shav) and "flattering divination" (mikesem chalaq) — the elimination of false prophecy is a mark of the messianic age (Zech 13:2-4) and of the era of the new covenant in which the Spirit is poured out directly upon the people (Joel 2:28-29; Acts 2:17). The juxtaposition of false vision with the true word of YHWH (v. 25) establishes the epistemological foundation for covenantal accountability.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: For I am YHWH; I will speak the word (dabar) that I will speak, and it shall be performed — it shall no longer be delayed; for in your days, O rebellious house, I will speak the word and perform it, declares the Lord YHWH."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The divine declaration "I am YHWH; I will speak the word that I will speak" is an assertion of absolute divine sovereignty over language, time, and event. The coupling of speech and performance (dabar + 'asah) reflects the Hebrew understanding of the divine word (dabar) as inherently efficacious — it does not return void (Isa 55:11). The urgency "in your days" closes the gap the cynics had opened with their proverb, confronting the exiles with imminent accountability.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying:

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Therefore say to them: Thus says the Lord YHWH: None of my words shall be delayed any longer; the word that I speak, it shall be performed, declares the Lord YHWH."
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This final verse of the chapter constitutes a formal divine seal on the entire unit, functioning as a rhetorical inclusio with the opening divine-word formula. The threefold repetition of the dabar-fulfillment motif (vv. 23, 25, 28) creates a climactic emphasis on the reliability and imminence of the prophetic word — no word of YHWH shall be delayed. This stands as a direct rebuttal to all three expressions of prophetic skepticism encountered in the chapter.

words.g: to/for a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: "Son of man, prophesy against the prophets of Israel who prophesy, and say to those who prophesy from their own hearts: Hear the word of YHWH!"

words.g: the a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: Thus says the Lord YHWH: "Woe to the foolish prophets who follow their own spirit (ruach), and have seen nothing!"
verses.note: HEBREW: "Foolish" (nabal, H5036) denotes moral and spiritual emptiness rather than mere intellectual deficiency — a willful rejection of wisdom and God's order. "Their own spirit (ruach, H7307)" contrasts starkly with the divine ruach that animates true prophecy (Ezek 2:2; 11:5). These prophets generate visions from internal impulse rather than divine commission, the ancient prophetic counterfeit. THEOLOGICAL: The criterion for authentic prophecy in the Hebrew canon is not charismatic experience but conformity to the word already spoken (Deut 18:20-22). False prophets are dangerous precisely because their experience feels real to them.

verses.flow: "Like foxes among ruins are your prophets, O Israel."

verses.flow: "You have not gone up into the breaches, nor have you built up a wall for the house of Israel to stand in battle on the day of YHWH."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The imagery of "going up into the breaches" (perets, H6556) and building a "wall" (gader, H1447) depicts the intercessory and protective role of the true prophet. Moses is the supreme OT exemplar of this calling (Ps 106:23). False prophets abandon the covenantal watchman role entirely, leaving the nation undefended. TYPOLOGY: Christ as the true intercessor stands in the ultimate breach, making intercession for transgressors (Isa 53:12; Heb 7:25), fulfilling what every faithful prophet could only foreshadow.

verses.flow: "And my hand shall be against the prophets who see false visions and who utter lying divinations. They shall not be in the council (sod) of my people, nor be enrolled in the register of the house of Israel, nor shall they enter the land of Israel. And you shall know that I am the Lord YHWH."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Council (sod, H5475)" is the technical term for the divine assembly — the heavenly court where authentic prophets receive their commission and word (Jer 23:18, 22; Amos 3:7; 1 Kgs 22:19-23). To be excluded from the sod of YHWH is to lack true prophetic standing. THEOLOGICAL: The threefold exclusion — from the sod, from the register, and from the land — constitutes a comprehensive covenant curse, reversing the privileges of Israel's election. The "register" (ketav, H3791) alludes to the book of the living (Exod 32:32; Ps 69:28).

verses.flow: "Precisely because they have led my people astray, saying, 'Peace (shalom),' when there is no shalom, and when one builds a wall, behold, they plaster it over with whitewash —"
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shalom (H7965)" in its full semantic range denotes wholeness, completeness, and covenantal well-being — not merely the absence of conflict. The false prophets reduce shalom to a political slogan detached from covenant faithfulness. "Whitewash (taphel, H8602)" refers to a thin coating of inferior plaster that conceals structural weakness without remedying it. THEOLOGICAL: The whitewashed wall becomes a powerful metaphor for religion that maintains the appearance of divine favor while the covenantal foundation has crumbled. Jesus echoes this imagery in his condemnation of the scribes and Pharisees (Matt 23:27).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: "say to those who plaster it with whitewash that it shall fall! There will come a flooding rain, and you, O great hailstones, shall fall, and a stormy wind shall burst forth."
"And behold, when the wall has fallen, will it not be said to you: 'Where is the plastering with which you plastered it?'"

verses.flow: Therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: "I will cause a stormy wind to burst forth in my fury, and there shall be a flooding rain in my anger, and great hailstones in my wrath to consume it."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The imagery of wind, flooding rain (geshem shoteף), and hailstones (avanim elgavish) evokes both literal Levantine storm phenomena and the ancient mythological association of storm with divine judgment. YHWH's storm theophany appears throughout the prophets and Psalms as an expression of his sovereign warrior power (Ps 18:7-15; Nah 1:3). THEOLOGICAL: The triple repetition — fury, anger, wrath — is a deliberate rhetorical intensification underscoring the irreversibility of divine judgment against those who weaponize his name.

verses.flow: "And say: Thus says the Lord YHWH: Woe to those who sew bands upon all wrists, and make veils for the heads of persons of every stature in order to hunt souls! Will you hunt the souls of my people and keep your own souls alive?"
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "bands" (kesatot, H3704) and "veils" (mispashot, H4555) are otherwise unattested terms in the Hebrew Bible, suggesting specialized instruments of magical practice. Ancient Near Eastern parallels from Mesopotamia include binding and releasing rituals involving cords and coverings used to manipulate fate and ensnare persons. THEOLOGICAL: The practice of "hunting souls (nefashot)" for profit inverts the pastoral calling entirely. These women function as spiritual predators, enslaving people through superstition while claiming divine authority.

verses.flow: "And you have profaned me among my people for handfuls of barley and for pieces of bread, putting to death souls who should not die and keeping alive souls who should not live, by your lying to my people who listen to lies."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Souls (nefashot, H5315)" here carries its full anthropological weight — the living persons themselves, not merely their immaterial components. The profaning of YHWH "among my people" (be-ammi) highlights the covenantal dimension of the offense: false prophecy desecrates the holy name within the very community that bears it. THEOLOGICAL: The exchange of human lives for "handfuls of barley and pieces of bread" recalls the merchants of Amos 8:6 who sell the poor for a pair of sandals — judgment for the monetization of sacred office.

verses.flow: "Because you have disheartened the righteous with falsehood, though I have brought no grief upon him, and you have strengthened the hands of the wicked, so that he does not turn from his wicked way and thereby save his life —"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse identifies two simultaneous spiritual crimes with inverse effects: the disheartening of the righteous through false condemnation, and the emboldening of the wicked through false reassurance. Together they constitute a comprehensive inversion of prophetic function — the true prophet is to comfort the afflicted and afflict the comfortable (Isa 40:1-2; Amos 5:21-24). TYPOLOGY: The pattern of strengthening wicked hands and discouraging the righteous anticipates the condemnation of Israel's leaders in the NT (Matt 23:13), who shut the kingdom against those entering and do not enter themselves.

verses.flow: "therefore you shall no longer see false visions, nor shall you practice divination. And I will deliver my people out of your hand. And you shall know that I am YHWH."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chapter closes with the covenant recognition formula "you shall know that I am YHWH" (yeda'tem ki-ani YHWH), which functions as Ezekiel's signature refrain, occurring over sixty times in the book. It signals that even the judgment of false prophets serves the ultimate divine purpose of self-disclosure and covenantal reestablishment. HEBREW: The cessation of "seeing false visions" and "practicing divination (qesem, H7081)" corresponds to the positive promise of a new prophetic community animated by the Spirit (ruach) of YHWH himself (Ezek 36:27; 39:29; Joel 2:28-29), whose ultimate fulfillment is in Christ (Acts 2:16-18).

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying,

"Son of man, these men have set up their idols (gillulim) upon their heart, and the stumbling block of their iniquity they have placed before their face. Shall I be inquired of (darash) at all by them?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Gillulim" (Strong's H1544) is a contemptuous term for idols, likely derived from the root meaning "dung pellets" or "logs." Ezekiel uses this term more than any other biblical author (39 times), and its scatological connotation underscores the utter defilement idolatry brings. "Darash" (H1875) means to seek or inquire, often used of consulting YHWH through prophetic means. The irony is devastating: men polluted with idolatry seek divine counsel while their hearts remain devoted to false gods.

verses.flow: Therefore speak to them and say to them: Thus says the Lord YHWH — Any man of the house of Israel who sets up his idols upon his heart and places the stumbling block of his iniquity before his face, and comes to the prophet — I, YHWH, will answer him who comes, according to the multitude of his idols,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse establishes a principle of divine judgment through the very act of inquiry — YHWH will answer the idolater "according to the multitude of his idols," meaning his own spiritual condition becomes the lens through which any divine response is filtered. This is a form of judicial hardening (cf. Rom 1:24-28), where God gives the rebellious what their hearts already desire, confirming them in their apostasy rather than rescuing them from it.

verses.flow: And as for the prophet, if he is deceived and speaks a word, I, YHWH, have deceived that prophet; and I will stretch out my hand against him, and will destroy him from the midst of my people Israel.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The declaration that YHWH "deceived" (patah, H6601) the false prophet is one of the most difficult theological statements in Ezekiel. The verb means to entice or persuade, and parallels texts like 1 Kings 22:20-23 where YHWH permits a lying spirit. This is not divine authorship of deception in an absolute sense, but a judicial act — YHWH withdraws restraint and allows the false prophet who already desires to deceive to be confirmed in that path, thereby bearing full accountability (cf. 2 Thess 2:11).

verses.flow: so that the house of Israel may no longer go astray from following me, and may no longer defile themselves with all their transgressions; and they shall be my people and I will be their God — declaration of the Lord YHWH."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The covenant formula "they shall be my people and I will be their God" echoes the Abrahamic and Mosaic covenants (Gen 17:7-8; Exod 6:7; Lev 26:12) and anticipates the New Covenant articulation in Jeremiah 31:33 and Ezekiel 36:28. Its fulfillment is found in Christ who, as the mediator of the new covenant, reconstitutes a purified people for YHWH (2 Cor 6:16; Rev 21:3). The goal of judgment is always restored covenant relationship.

verses.flow: "Son of man, when a land sins against me by acting faithlessly (ma'al), and I stretch out my hand against it and break its supply of bread and send famine upon it and cut off from it man and beast,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ma'al" (H4603) denotes a treacherous, faithless act — often used of covenant violation or sacrilege against holy things (Lev 5:15; Num 5:12). The fourfold judgment pattern (famine, wild beasts, sword, pestilence) mirrors the covenant curses of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28, indicating that Jerusalem's fate is the direct outworking of the Mosaic covenant's sanctions against sustained national rebellion.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: even if these three men were in its midst — Noah, Daniel (Dani'el), and Job — they, by their righteousness (tsedaqah), would deliver only their own souls, declares the Lord YHWH.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The mention of "Daniel" (Dani'el) here alongside Noah and Job — all non-Israelite or pre-Israelite righteous figures in ancient tradition — has generated significant scholarly discussion. Some argue this refers to the ancient Ugaritic figure "Dnil" known from the Aqhat epic, a legendary just ruler, rather than Ezekiel's younger contemporary Daniel. However, the canonical Daniel was already renowned for wisdom and righteousness by this point in the exile (cf. Dan 1-2), and most traditional interpreters identify them as one. The three figures span antediluvian (Noah), patriarchal (Job), and contemporary (Daniel) epochs, universalizing the principle.

even if these three men were in its midst — as I live, declares the Lord YHWH — they would deliver neither sons nor daughters; they alone would be delivered, but the land would become desolate.

verses.flow: Or if I send pestilence into that land and pour out my wrath upon it in blood, to cut off from it man and beast,
even if Noah, Daniel, and Job were in its midst — as I live, declares the Lord YHWH — they would deliver neither son nor daughter; they would deliver only their own souls by their righteousness.
For thus says the Lord YHWH: How much more when I send my four dreadful judgments upon Jerusalem — sword, and famine, and wild beasts, and pestilence — to cut off from it man and beast!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "four dreadful judgments" (literally "four evil judgments," arba mishpatim ra'im) applied to Jerusalem represent a totality of divine wrath, recalling the covenant curse structure of Leviticus 26:21-26 and paralleling the four horsemen imagery of Revelation 6:1-8. The intensification — "how much more" — makes clear that Jerusalem is receiving a judgment exceeding even that of a generic sinful nation, precisely because of her greater covenant privilege and therefore greater covenant accountability (cf. Amos 3:2; Luke 12:48).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Yet behold, there shall be left in it a remnant who are brought out, both sons and daughters. Behold, they will come out to you, and you will see their way and their deeds; and you will be comforted (nacham) concerning the disaster that I have brought upon Jerusalem, concerning all that I have brought upon it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Nacham" (H5162), here rendered "comforted," carries a complex semantic range: it can mean to be comforted, to regret, to relent, or to have compassion. In this context the survivors' wickedness — visible in their "way and their deeds" — will paradoxically bring comfort to the exiles not through pleasant news but by vindicating YHWH's justice. Seeing that the remnant deserved judgment confirms that God acted rightly. This is comfort through theodicy, not through relief. A remnant theology emerges: even in total judgment, preservation of a seed remains (cf. Isa 6:13; Rom 9:27).

verses.flow: They will comfort you when you see their way and their deeds, and you shall know that it was not without cause that I have done all that I have done in it — declaration of the Lord YHWH."

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying:
"Son of man, what advantage is the wood of the vine over all wood — the branch that is among the trees of the forest?
verses.note: HEBREW: The vine (gefen, H1612) was Israel's most celebrated national symbol, representing the covenant people chosen and planted by YHWH (Ps 80:8-16; Isa 5:1-7). The rhetorical question here turns the symbol on its head — the vine's value lies entirely in its fruit; apart from fruitfulness, its wood is inferior even to common forest timber, being too soft and knotty for construction or craftsmanship. This inverts Israel's presumed status: election guarantees nothing apart from covenant faithfulness.

words.m: particle + verb qal passive imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Is wood taken from it to make anything? Do men take from it a peg to hang any vessel upon?

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Behold, it is given to the fire for fuel; the fire devours both its ends, and its middle is charred. Is it useful for any work?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The image of wood charred at both ends and the middle (the Hebrew bayit, "between the two ends," is vivid) heightens the picture of total uselessness. Fire here is not purifying (as in Isa 6) but consuming and final. The passage anticipates the Babylonian siege as YHWH's judicial instrument. TYPOLOGY: Jesus employs the exact same vine-and-fruitlessness motif in John 15:1-6, where branches that bear no fruit are gathered and burned — a direct intertextual echo of Ezekiel 15.

verses.flow: Therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: As the wood of the vine among the trees of the forest, which I have given to the fire for fuel, so I have given up the inhabitants of Jerusalem.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The formula "thus says the Lord YHWH" (koh amar Adonai YHWH) appears over 120 times in Ezekiel, functioning as a covenantal lawsuit declaration. The comparison is explicit: Jerusalem is to YHWH what charred vine-wood is to a craftsman — fit only for burning. This shocks the reader who presumed Jerusalem's election was a guarantee of inviolability (cf. Jer 7:4). The covenant relationship intensifies rather than removes judgment when faithfulness is absent.

verses.flow: And I will set my face against them; from the fire they go forth, yet the fire shall devour them. And you shall know that I am YHWH, when I set my face against them.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "I will set my face against them" (natatti et-panay bahem) is the judicial posture of divine opposition, the inverse of the Aaronic blessing "YHWH make His face shine upon you" (Num 6:25). The repetition within the verse — they escape fire only to be consumed by fire — suggests there is no refuge from the divine decree. The recognition formula "you shall know that I am YHWH" (yeda'tem ki-ani YHWH) occurs over 70 times in Ezekiel and functions as the ultimate purpose of both judgment and restoration.

verses.flow: And I will make the land desolate, because they have acted faithlessly, declares the Lord YHWH."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Acted faithlessly" renders ma'al (H4603), a term denoting covenant treachery or sacrilegious breach of trust, often used of violations against YHWH's holiness (Lev 5:15; 1 Chr 10:13). The declaration "the land desolate" (shamemah, H8077) closes the short parable with legal finality. HISTORICAL: The desolation of the land fulfills the Levitical covenant curses (Lev 26:31-33) and sets the theological stage for Ezekiel's extended allegories of Jerusalem's unfaithfulness in chapters 16 and 23.

verses.flow: and say, Thus says the Lord YHWH to Jerusalem: Your origin and your birth are from the land of the Canaanite; your father was an Amorite and your mother a Hittite.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The identification of Jerusalem's "father" as Amorite and "mother" as Hittite is theologically provocative — not literal ethnic genealogy, but a reminder that Canaan's pre-Israelite inhabitants were morally corrupt peoples under divine judgment (Gen 15:16). Hittites and Amorites represent the pagan substrate beneath Israel's sacred calling. This also alludes to the mixed origins of the city itself, as Jerusalem was a Jebusite stronghold (2 Sam 5:6).

verses.flow: And as for your birth, on the day you were born your navel cord was not cut, and in water you were not washed for cleansing, and with salt you were not salted, and in swaddling cloths you were not wrapped.

words.m: conjunction + verb hophal sequential imperfect 2nd fem sing

verses.flow: No eye pitied you to do any of these things to you, to have compassion upon you; but you were cast out upon the face of the open field, in the loathing of your soul (nephesh), on the day you were born.
verses.note: HEBREW: "nephesh" (נֶפֶשׁ, H5315) here rendered "soul" in its sense of vital life-force. The phrase "in the loathing of your nephesh" (beshiquts nafshek) conveys that the infant was cast out with utter contempt for its very life. The practice of infant exposure was documented in the ANE and described in Hittite and Egyptian texts. HISTORICAL: Infant exposure of unwanted children was practiced among Canaanite and surrounding cultures, making this image culturally legible to Ezekiel's audience.

verses.flow: And I passed by you and saw you, and behold, your time was the time of love; and I spread my skirt (kanaf) over you and covered your nakedness. And I swore to you and entered into covenant with you, declares the Lord YHWH, and you became mine.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Kanaf" (כָּנָף, H3671) means "wing" or "skirt/hem of a garment" — spreading the kanaf over one was a gesture of betrothal and protection, echoing Ruth 3:9. The entry into "covenant" (berit, H1285) formalizes the Sinai relationship as a marriage covenant. THEOLOGICAL: This verse encapsulates the Sinai covenant as a divine marriage, a concept also developed in Hosea 2 and Jeremiah 2. The covenantal framework here is not merely legal but intensely relational and personal.

verses.flow: And you were adorned with gold and silver, and your clothing was fine linen and silk and embroidered cloth; fine flour and honey and oil you ate; and you became exceedingly beautiful and advanced to royal dignity.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The lavish gifts catalogued in vv. 10-13 represent the blessings of the Mosaic covenant — the land, the temple, the kingship of David — all understood as divine dowry bestowed upon Israel. "Royal dignity" (lit. "you prospered unto kingship," vatitzkhi limlacha) points to the Davidic monarchy as the apex of covenantal blessing.

verses.flow: And your name went out among the nations on account of your beauty, for it was perfect through my splendor which I had bestowed upon you, declares the Lord YHWH.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The beauty and renown of Jerusalem points proleptically to the eschatological Jerusalem, whose glory derives entirely from YHWH's indwelling presence (Rev 21:11, 23). The phrase "my splendor which I had bestowed upon you" makes clear that all beauty is derivative — Jerusalem had no inherent glory apart from YHWH.

verses.flow: And you took your sons and your daughters whom you had borne to me and sacrificed them to be devoured. Was your harlotry too small a thing,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Child sacrifice to Molech and other Canaanite deities is attested archaeologically at sites like Carthage (tophet sites) and is condemned throughout the Hebrew Bible (Lev 18:21; 2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 7:31). Ezekiel frames this as killing children who belonged to YHWH — "your sons and daughters whom you had borne to me" — heightening the covenantal treachery. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "idols" (gillulim) to whom children were sacrificed likely represent territorial deities — the divine beings assigned to the nations in Deut 32:8-9 who became the objects of illicit Israelite worship.

verses.flow: And in all your abominations and your harlotries you did not remember the days of your youth, when you were naked and bare, wallowing in your blood.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The refrain "you did not remember" (lo zakarti) is central to the rhetorical structure of the chapter. In covenant theology, "remembering" (zakar, H2142) is not merely cognitive recall but active covenant loyalty and faithful response. Israel's failure to remember constitutes a fundamental covenantal rupture.

verses.flow: Thus says the Lord YHWH: Because your lust (nechoshet) was poured out and your nakedness uncovered in your harlotries with your lovers, and with all the idols of your abominations, and because of the blood of your children that you gave to them,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Nechoshet" (נְחֻשְׁתֵּךְ, H5178) is debated — some render it "bronze/brass" (suggesting monetary lust or shamelessness), others "lust" or "filthiness." The LXX reads "your fornication was poured out." The root likely carries the sense of base, debased desire — what is base and shameful. THEOLOGICAL: The triad of sins addressed here — idolatry, bloodshed, and sexual immorality — mirrors the prophetic indictment of Amos, Hosea, and Micah, and anticipates the NT's identification of these as the marks of pagan corruption (Rom 1:18-32).

verses.flow: therefore behold, I will gather all your lovers with whom you took pleasure, and all those you loved, together with all those you hated; and I will gather them against you from every side and will uncover your nakedness to them, so that they will see all your nakedness.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The gathering of "all your lovers" as instruments of divine judgment reflects a pattern found throughout the prophets wherein YHWH uses pagan nations — who themselves serve other divine beings (Deut 32:8; Ps 82) — as agents of covenant enforcement. The exposed nakedness before enemies echoes the shame of covenant curse (Deut 28:48).

verses.flow: Because you did not remember the days of your youth and have enraged me with all these things — therefore behold, I also have returned your conduct upon your head, declares the Lord YHWH. Have you not committed lewdness in addition to all your abominations?
Behold, everyone who uses proverbs will use this proverb against you: "Like mother, like daughter."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The proverb "Like mother, like daughter" (ka'em kitah, lit. "as the mother, so her daughter") frames the entire genealogical argument of vv. 44-52. Israel's sin is not aberrant but systemic — a corrupted inheritance from pagan origins (v. 3) never fully renounced. This does not excuse the sin but deepens the indictment.

verses.flow: You are the daughter of your mother, who loathed her husband and her children; and you are the sister of your sisters, who loathed their husbands and their children. Your mother was a Hittite and your father an Amorite.
And your elder sister is Samaria, who dwelt with her daughters to your north; and your younger sister, who dwelt to your south, is Sodom with her daughters.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Samaria (the northern kingdom of Israel) fell to Assyria in 722 BC (2 Kgs 17) and Sodom was destroyed in primordial judgment (Gen 19). Placing Jerusalem in sisterhood with both is a devastating rhetorical move — Jerusalem is being measured against two paradigmatic cases of divine judgment and found worse than both.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 2nd fem sing

verses.flow: Yet you did not walk in their ways or do according to their abominations. As if that were too little, you became more corrupt than they in all your ways.
As I live, declares the Lord YHWH, your sister Sodom and her daughters have not done as you and your daughters have done.
Behold, this was the guilt (avon) of your sister Sodom: pride, fullness of bread, and careless ease were in her and in her daughters, and she did not strengthen the hand of the poor and needy.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is frequently cited in discussions of the "sin of Sodom." Ezekiel does not limit Sodom's guilt to sexual transgression but identifies pride, material satiety, and indifference to the poor as primary sins. This does not exclude Genesis 19's account but contextualizes the moral failure within a broader covenantal-ethical framework (cf. Matt 11:23-24; Isa 1:10).

verses.flow: Yet I will remember my covenant with you in the days of your youth, and I will establish for you an everlasting covenant (berit olam).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Berit olam" (בְּרִית עוֹלָם, H1285/H5769) — "everlasting covenant" — is the same formula used for the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 17:7), the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 23:5), and the new covenant (Jer 32:40; Isa 55:3). TYPOLOGY: Despite Jerusalem's total covenantal failure catalogued in this chapter, YHWH pledges to remember and restore — not on the basis of Israel's faithfulness but solely on divine initiative. This is a direct anticipation of the new covenant enacted in Christ (Heb 8:8-12; Jer 31:31-34), in which YHWH's faithfulness overcomes Israel's faithlessness.

verses.flow: And you shall remember your ways and be ashamed when you receive your sisters, the elder and the younger; and I will give them to you as daughters, but not on account of your covenant.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The reception of "sisters" (Samaria and Sodom) as daughters to Jerusalem in the restored order suggests an eschatological ingathering that transcends ethnic Israel — a foreshadowing of the nations being incorporated into the covenant people (Eph 2:11-22; Rom 11:17). The caveat "not on account of your covenant" (lo mibritekh) eliminates any ground for human boasting.

verses.flow: so that you may remember and be confounded, and never open your mouth again because of your shame, when I make atonement for you for all that you have done, declares the Lord YHWH."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chapter closes not with triumphalism but with speechless shame — the restored community is silenced by the weight of divine grace received despite total unworthiness. "Atonement" (kapper, H3722) — the same root as kapporet (the mercy seat) — signals that only YHWH's own covering act can resolve the covenantal breach. TYPOLOGY: This atoning act points forward to the definitive atonement of Christ, whose blood covers the full weight of Israel's (and humanity's) covenantal infidelity (Rom 3:25; 1 John 2:2), leaving the forgiven without any word to say in their own defense.

verses.flow: and say, Thus says the Lord YHWH: A great eagle with great wings and long pinions, full of feathers of many colors, came to Lebanon and took the top of the cedar.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term translated "riddle" is chidah (Strong's H2420), referring to a dark or enigmatic saying, a puzzle requiring interpretation. "Parable" is mashal (H4912), a comparison or allegory. The double genre signals that ch. 17 operates on two levels simultaneously — political allegory and prophetic oracle. The "great eagle" (nesher gadol) with "great wings" (kanaph gadol) and "many colors" (riqdamah) likely evokes Babylonian royal iconography, the lamassu or eagle-headed figures of ANE art.

verses.flow: He plucked off the topmost of its young twigs and brought it to a land of trade; in a city of merchants he set it.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "A land of trade" and "a city of merchants" almost certainly refers to Babylon, whose commercial dominance in the ancient Near East is well-attested in cuneiform records. The "top of the cedar" (rosh ha-erez) represents King Jehoiachin, taken into Babylonian exile in 597 B.C. (2 Kings 24:10-16). The imagery of transplanting a royal shoot reflects ANE political practice of hostage-taking and vassal installation.

verses.flow: And he took one of the royal offspring and made a covenant (berit) with him, putting him under oath; and the mighty of the land he took away,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "covenant" (berit, Strong's H1285) established between Nebuchadnezzar and Zedekiah was a suzerainty treaty, solemnized by oath, likely invoking YHWH as divine guarantor (cf. v. 19). YHWH's subsequent judgment treats the breaking of this covenant as treachery against himself — a profound covenantal theology that regards oaths sworn in YHWH's name as binding before him regardless of the political context in which they were made.

verses.flow: But he rebelled against him by sending his ambassadors to Egypt, that they might give him horses and a large army. Will he prosper? Will he who does such things escape? Can he break covenant (berit) and yet escape?
As I live, declares the Lord YHWH, surely in the place of the king who made him king, whose oath he despised and whose covenant (berit) he broke — in Babylon he shall die.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The divine oath formula "As I live" (chai ani, H2416 + H589) is the strongest form of divine self-affirmation in the Hebrew Bible, replacing the standard oath "by YHWH" with YHWH himself as the oath-taker. This rhetorical inversion underscores that the broken covenant was ultimately a covenant with YHWH, not merely a political pact. Zedekiah's death in Babylon is historically confirmed; he was blinded and imprisoned there until his death (2 Kings 25:7; Jer 52:11).

verses.flow: Therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: As I live, surely my oath that he despised and my covenant (berit) that he broke, I will return upon his own head.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH explicitly identifies the suzerainty oath as "my oath" and the treaty as "my covenant," confirming that human covenants solemnized in YHWH's name carry divine sanction. This is consistent with the Deuteronomic theology of oaths (Deut 23:21-23) and reflects the ancient belief that oath-breaking is a form of sacrilege against the deity invoked.

Ps 9:15, Job 19:6). YHWH here acts as cosmic sovereign executing judgment on a covenant-breaker through the instrument of Babylonian military power — Nebuchadnezzar as unwitting agent of the divine court's decree.

verses.flow: Thus says the Lord YHWH: I myself will take a shoot from the lofty top of the cedar and will set it out; from the topmost of its young twigs I will pluck a tender one, and I myself will plant it on a high and lofty mountain.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse inaugurates one of Ezekiel's most significant messianic oracles. The divine "I myself" (ani, emphatic) marks a direct act of YHWH in contrast to the eagles of vv. 3-7. The "tender twig" (yoneket raqqah) plucked from the cedar's crown and transplanted by YHWH directly anticipates the Davidic Messiah of Isaiah 11:1 (the "shoot from the stump of Jesse") and Jeremiah 23:5 (the "righteous Branch"). The NT understands Jesus as this royal shoot (Luke 1:32-33; Rev 22:16: "the root and offspring of David").

verses.flow: On the mountain height of Israel I will plant it, and it shall bear branches and produce fruit and become a noble cedar. And under it shall dwell every bird of every wing; in the shade of its branches they shall dwell.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of the great cedar on "the mountain height of Israel" under which "every bird of every wing" finds shelter is a direct messianic-eschatological vision. The universality of shelter — "every bird of every wing" — prefigures the Gentile inclusion in the messianic kingdom (cf. Matt 13:31-32, where Jesus cites the analogous mustard tree parable; Mark 4:32; Dan 4:12 for the ANE background of the sheltering world-tree). The mountain of Israel as the seat of divine planting evokes Zion theology throughout the Psalter.

verses.flow: And all the trees of the field shall know that I am YHWH; I bring low the high tree, I make high the low tree, I dry up the green tree, and I make the dry tree flourish. I am YHWH; I have spoken, and I will do it."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing declaration employs four antithetical pairs — high/low, low/high, green/dry, dry/flourishing — constituting a reversal-of-fortunes formula central to prophetic eschatology. YHWH's sovereignty over the "trees of the field" (all nations and their rulers, per the allegory) culminates in the double self-identification: "I am YHWH; I have spoken, and I will do it." The performative certainty of the divine word (dabar) as both speech and act is foundational to Ezekielic theology (cf. Ezek 12:25, 24:14; Isa 55:10-11).

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying:
"What do you mean by using this proverb concerning the land of Israel, saying: 'The fathers eat sour grapes, and the teeth of the sons are set on edge'?"
"As I live," declares the Lord YHWH, "surely you shall no longer use this proverb in Israel."
"Behold, all souls (nephesh) are mine; as the soul of the father, so also the soul of the son is mine — the soul that sins, it shall die."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The declaration "all souls are mine" (kol-ha-nephesh li) establishes YHWH's sovereign ownership over every life as the basis for individual accountability. This verse is the theological hinge of the chapter, dismantling the fatalistic misreading of Exodus 20:5 (generational consequences for covenant unfaithfulness) into a denial of personal moral responsibility. The statement "the soul that sins, it shall die" (ha-nephesh ha-chote't hi tamut) is the foundational axiom of Ezekiel's entire argument in this chapter. (Deut 24:16; Rom 6:23)

verses.flow: "But if a man is righteous and does justice (mishpat) and righteousness (tzedakah),"
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Verses 5-9 constitute the first of three legal case studies (Hebrew: mashal-style casuistic form), presenting the righteous man as the baseline. The two-word summary "justice and righteousness" (mishpat u-tzedakah) is a covenant hendiadys appearing throughout the prophets as the defining characteristic of covenant fidelity. (Amos 5:24; Isa 1:27; Jer 22:3)

verses.flow: "and does not eat upon the mountains, nor lift up his eyes to the idols of the house of Israel, nor defile his neighbor's wife, nor approach a woman in her menstrual impurity,"
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Eating upon the mountains" refers to participation in idolatrous high-place sacrificial meals, a syncretic practice common in Canaan and condemned throughout the Deuteronomistic tradition. The pairing of cultic and ethical violations (idolatry alongside sexual ethics and social justice) reflects the integrated nature of Torah faithfulness — covenant loyalty is inseparable from communal moral order. (Ezek 6:3-4; Lev 19:26; Deut 12:2)

verses.flow: "and oppresses no one, but restores the pledge to the debtor, commits no robbery, gives his bread to the hungry, and covers the naked with a garment,"
"and does not lend at interest (neshek) nor take any increase, who withholds his hand from iniquity, executes true judgment between man and man,"
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "neshek" (H5391, from nashak, "to bite") refers to interest charged on a loan, metaphorically conceived as the bite of a serpent devouring the borrower's livelihood. Its pairing with "tarbit" or "marbit" (increase, profit) forms a standard doublet in priestly and prophetic literature condemning exploitative lending. Torah explicitly forbade charging interest to fellow Israelites (Lev 25:36-37; Deut 23:19), making this a covenant-specific prohibition rooted in the social vision of Jubilee economics.

verses.flow: "lends at interest and takes increase — shall he then live? He shall not live. He has done all these abominations; he shall surely die; his blood shall be upon himself."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The formula "his blood shall be upon himself" (damo bo yihyeh) is a formal legal declaration of self-incurred guilt, meaning the penalty of death falls upon the guilty party alone and cannot be transferred. This phrase recurs in Ezekiel as a technical term for juridical self-responsibility (Ezek 33:4-5). The threefold escalating structure — righteous father, wicked son, repentant grandson — is a rabbinic-style legal argument (din) designed to demonstrate that YHWH's justice is purely individual and present-tense. (Josh 2:19; Acts 18:6)

verses.flow: "Yet you say: 'Why does the son not bear the iniquity of the father?' When the son has done what is just and right, and has kept all my statutes and done them, he shall surely live."

"The soul (nephesh) that sins, it shall die. The son shall not bear the iniquity of the father, nor the father bear the iniquity of the son. The righteousness of the righteous shall be upon himself, and the wickedness of the wicked shall be upon himself."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the theological summit of the chapter, a formal legal declaration of the principle of individual retribution (Hebrew: gemul — recompense). The deliberate symmetry of the verse ("son shall not bear... father shall not bear") directly addresses and corrects the popular misapplication of the Decalogue's generational language (Exod 20:5), clarifying that covenantal consequences for national apostasy do not override individual moral accountability before YHWH. (Deut 24:16; 2 Kgs 14:6; Jer 31:29-30)

verses.flow: "Do I have any pleasure (chaphetz) in the death of the wicked," declares the Lord YHWH, "and not rather that he should turn from his way and live?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical question "Do I have any pleasure (chaphetz) in the death of the wicked?" anticipates and amplifies the closing declaration of verse 32. The Hebrew "chaphetz" (H2654) denotes deep delight or desire, not mere approval — YHWH's emotional posture is one of active longing for repentance, not satisfaction in judgment. This verse is among the most explicit Old Testament declarations of divine salvific intent toward the wicked, undergirding the New Testament proclamation that God "desires all people to be saved." (Ezek 33:11; 1 Tim 2:4; 2 Pet 3:9)

verses.flow: "But when the righteous man turns away from his righteousness and commits iniquity, and does according to all the abominations that the wicked man does, shall he live? All his righteous deeds that he has done shall not be remembered; in his treachery which he has committed, and in his sin which he has sinned — in them he shall die."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The sobering counterpart to verse 21, this verse establishes that apostasy from righteousness results in the same death as persistent wickedness. Prior righteous deeds do not accumulate as a permanent ledger credit that neutralizes later rebellion. This reflects Ezekiel's pastoral concern for presumptuous security among those who trusted in inherited covenantal status or past moral record — a direct challenge to exilic Israelite self-understanding. (1 Cor 10:12; Heb 6:4-6)

verses.flow: "Yet you say: 'The way of the Lord is not just (takan).' Hear now, O house of Israel: Is my way not just? Is it not your ways that are not just?"
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "takan" (H8505, "to be just, right, weighed, measured") carries the imagery of a balance scale or plumb line used to assess whether something is true and level. The accusation that YHWH's way is "lo yitaken" (not measured/weighed correctly) reflects a popular theodicy complaint — that YHWH's governance of the world was arbitrary or unfair. Ezekiel turns the charge back: it is Israel's moral trajectories that are crooked, not YHWH's juridical standards. (Prov 16:2; Dan 5:27)

verses.flow: "Therefore I will judge you, O house of Israel, every one according to his ways," declares the Lord YHWH. "Repent and turn from all your transgressions, so that iniquity may not be your ruin (mikshol)."
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "mikshol" (H4383, "stumbling block, ruin") derives from the root kashal, meaning to stumble or fall. YHWH warns that iniquity itself becomes the structural obstacle that causes the sinner's collapse — not an external punishment imposed arbitrarily, but the self-destructive consequence of transgression. The call to "repent and turn" (shuvu v'hashivu) uses the Hebrew "shuv" twice in different conjugations, intensifying the urgency of the summons to radical reorientation. (Ezek 14:3-4; Rev 2:5)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: "Cast away from you all your transgressions by which you have transgressed, and make yourselves a new heart and a new spirit (ruach); for why will you die, O house of Israel?"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The command "make yourselves a new heart and a new spirit (ruach)" stands in deliberate theological tension with Ezekiel's own later oracle in chapter 36:26, where YHWH declares "I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit within you." Together these passages reveal the dual structure of covenant renewal: human responsibility to turn and divine grace to transform. The New Covenant fulfillment occurs through the Messiah's work and the indwelling of the Holy Spirit. (Ezek 36:26-27; Jer 31:31-34; 2 Cor 5:17)

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: "For I have no pleasure (chaphetz) in the death of anyone who dies," declares the Lord YHWH, "so turn, and live."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The final verse functions as a doxological conclusion and divine oath — YHWH's deepest will is not judgment but life. The repetition of "chaphetz" from verse 23 forms an inclusio bracketing the chapter's closing appeal, asserting the consistency of divine desire across all moral situations. The imperative "turn and live" (shuvu v'chiyu) is Ezekiel's gospel in miniature — repentance is possible, death is not inevitable, and YHWH actively summons the condemned toward life. (John 3:16-17; 1 Tim 2:4; 2 Pet 3:9)

verses.flow: And you, lift up a lamentation (qinah) for the princes of Israel,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Lamentation" (qinah, Strong's H7015) refers to a specific dirge-poem sung over the dead, characterized by a distinctive 3+2 meter in Hebrew poetry (the "qinah meter"). The imperative "lift up" (se'u) signals a formal public mourning. That this lament is addressed to "princes" (nesiim, H5387) rather than a single king is significant — the plural may encompass the entire Davidic dynasty now in collapse. STRUCTURE: The chapter is formally structured as a funeral song in two stanzas (vv. 2-9 and vv. 10-14), both employing extended allegory — first a lioness and her cubs, then a vine and its branches.

verses.flow: and say: What was your mother? A lioness! Among lions she crouched; in the midst of young lions she reared her cubs.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The mother lioness is almost certainly a figure for Israel itself — or more specifically, the royal house of Judah. The lion imagery echoes the tribal blessing of Judah in (Gen 49:9), where Judah is called a "lion's cub" (gur aryeh), the same term used here. The lioness crouching among lions evokes Judah's royal dignity before her fall. TYPOLOGY: The ultimate Lion of the tribe of Judah is the Messiah (Rev 5:5), making this passage a tragic contrast — the earthly lions who devoured men stand against the coming King who conquers on behalf of his people.

verses.flow: And she brought up one of her cubs; he became a young lion, and he learned to tear prey; he devoured men.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "one of her cubs" brought up here is almost universally identified with Jehoahaz (also called Shallum), son of Josiah, who reigned only three months in 609 BC before being deported to Egypt by Pharaoh Necho II (2 Kgs 23:31-34). "He learned to tear prey; he devoured men" likely reflects Jehoahaz's violent, oppressive reign, consistent with the prophetic condemnation in (Jer 22:11-12).

verses.flow: And the nations heard of him; he was caught in their pit, and they brought him with hooks to the land of Egypt.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "pit" (shuchah, H7882) and "hooks" (vavim, or here chachim, H2397) recall ANE hunting practices for large felines, wherein pits were dug and covered, and captured animals were dragged with metal hooks. The imagery pointedly compares the proud king to a trapped beast. Egypt's role in deposing Jehoahaz and exiling him to Riblah and then Egypt is recorded in (2 Kgs 23:33-34). He died in Egypt, never returning to the land.

verses.flow: And she saw that she had waited, that her hope was lost, and she took another of her cubs and made him a young lion.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Her hope was lost" (Hebrew: ki-avdah tiqvatah) is a stark covenantal phrase — the same root (tiqvah, H8615) used for hope and expectation. The mother's act of raising another cub reflects the dynastic succession, but the language of lost hope anticipates the full collapse of Davidic kingship. The true and living Hope of Israel will only be restored in the Messianic King (Jer 23:5-6).

verses.note: HISTORICAL: This second cub is most likely Jehoiachin (Jeconiah), who also reigned only three months (598-597 BC) before being deported to Babylon by Nebuchadnezzar (2 Kgs 24:8-15). Some interpreters identify him as Zedekiah, the last reigning king, but the deportation to Babylon in v. 9 more precisely fits Jehoiachin. The repetition of the phrase "he learned to tear prey" deliberately echoes v. 3, marking the pattern of dynastic failure.

verses.flow: And they put him in a cage with hooks, and brought him to the king of Babylon; they brought him into fortresses, so that his voice should no more be heard on the mountains of Israel.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "So that his voice should no more be heard on the mountains of Israel" is a devastating covenantal reversal. The mountains of Israel are the geographic heartland of the covenant people, associated with divine inheritance and royal proclamation. Silence on those mountains signals the complete suspension of the Davidic monarchy. This silence will not be broken until the one whose right it is comes to reign (Ezek 21:27; cf. Luke 1:32-33). HISTORICAL: "The king of Babylon" (melek Bavel) here is Nebuchadnezzar II. "Fortresses" (metsudot, H4686) may refer to the prison-fortress where Jehoiachin was held in Babylon, consistent with the cuneiform Weidner Tablets discovered in Babylon that record rations distributed to "Yaukin, king of Judah."

words.g: in/with blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: Your mother was like a vine in your vineyard, planted by the waters; fruitful and full of branches was she by reason of abundant waters.
verses.note: HEBREW: The shift from lion allegory to vine allegory (gefen, H1612) follows a well-established OT pattern of representing Israel as YHWH's vine (Ps 80:8-16; Isa 5:1-7; Hos 10:1). "Planted by the waters" (shatul al-mayim) echoes the imagery of Ps 1:3 and Jer 17:8, connoting covenant blessing and prosperity. "In your vineyard" (bekarmekha) is a reading preserved in several manuscripts; the MT has "in your blood" (bedamekha), which some scholars retain as an image of vitality or perhaps of violence — both readings have ancient support. STRUCTURE: This second stanza (vv. 10-14) is the more devastating of the two, ending not with mere exile but with the complete destruction of the vine from within.

verses.flow: And she had strong branches fit for the scepters of rulers; her stature was exalted among the thick boughs; she was seen in her height with the multitude of her branches.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Strong branches fit for the scepters of rulers" (mattot oz le-shivtei moshlim) directly associates the vine's branches with royal authority. The scepter (shevet, H7626) is the same word used in the royal blessing of (Gen 49:10): "The scepter shall not depart from Judah." The vine's former glory thus represents the full flourishing of the Davidic covenant. Its coming destruction makes the prophecy all the more poignant, pointing forward to the One in whom the scepter ultimately rests (cf. Num 24:17).

verses.flow: But she was plucked up in fury; she was cast down to the ground, and the east wind dried up her fruit; her strong branches were stripped off and withered; fire consumed them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The east wind" (ruach ha-qadim, H6921) dried up her fruit — the east wind from the Arabian desert is a recurring biblical symbol of divine judgment (cf. Hos 13:15; Gen 41:6). The plucking up "in fury" (be-chemah, H2534) is YHWH's covenantal wrath against covenant-breaking. Fire consuming the branches anticipates the final allegory in v. 14 where the fire comes from within the vine itself — an image of self-destruction through internal corruption and ungodly leadership.

verses.flow: And now she is planted in the wilderness, in a dry and thirsty land.

verses.flow: And fire has gone out from the rod of her branches; it has consumed her fruit, so that there remains in her no strong branch, no scepter for ruling. This is a lamentation (qinah), and it has become a lamentation.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The climactic and devastating note of v. 14 is that the fire consuming the vine came "from the rod of her branches" — that is, from within, from the royal house itself. This implicates the Davidic kings in the destruction of their own dynasty through their faithlessness. "No scepter for ruling" (ein-lah matteh-oz shevet le-moshel) declares the complete cessation of functional Davidic kingship, which will only be restored eschatologically in the Messiah (cf. Ezek 34:23-24; Luke 1:32-33). STRUCTURE: The repetition of qinah ("lamentation") in the final line — "it has become a lamentation" — creates a recursive closure: the nation has become the very subject of its own funeral song, a profound rhetorical and theological indictment.

verses.flow: And it came to pass in the seventh year, in the fifth month, on the tenth day of the month, that men of the elders of Israel came to inquire of YHWH, and they sat before me.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "seventh year" places this oracle in 591 BC, during Ezekiel's exile in Babylon. The elders coming to "inquire of YHWH" (darash, H1875) reflects the emerging institution of prophetic consultation when the temple was inaccessible. This same scenario opens chapter 14, indicating a recurring pattern of hypocritical consultation that YHWH rejects. (Ezek 14:1-3)

verses.flow: "Son of man, speak to the elders of Israel and say to them: Thus says the Lord YHWH: Is it to inquire of me that you come? As I live, I will not be inquired of by you," declares the Lord YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's refusal to be inquired of (darash, H1875) by these elders is stark and deliberate. The divine oath formula "as I live" (chai ani) signals the irrevocable nature of the divine response. God's refusal to answer is itself the answer — judgment upon a people whose outward piety masks persistent idolatry. (Ezek 14:3; Isa 1:15)

verses.flow: And say to them: Thus says the Lord YHWH: On the day when I chose Israel and lifted up my hand to the offspring of the house of Jacob and made myself known to them in the land of Egypt — when I lifted up my hand to them, saying, I am YHWH your God —
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "lifted up my hand" (nasa yadi) is the covenant oath gesture, used throughout this chapter (vv. 6, 15, 23, 28) as a legal idiom for solemn swearing. "Chose" (bachar, H977) signals divine election rooted in YHWH's sovereign will, not Israel's merit. This recapitulates covenant history in the style of Deut 26 and Ps 78. (Deut 7:6; Ps 78:12-53)

verses.flow: And I said to them: Cast away, each of you, the detestable things of his eyes, and do not defile yourselves with the idols of Egypt; I am YHWH your God.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The command to "cast away the detestable things of Egypt" implies that Israelite idolatry predated the Exodus — a theme confirmed by Josh 24:14 and echoed in Amos 5:25-26. Egyptian gods such as Apis and Hathor were apparently worshipped alongside YHWH. This challenges any idealized view of pre-Sinai Israel. (Josh 24:14; Amos 5:25)

verses.flow: But I acted for the sake of my name, that it should not be profaned in the eyes of the nations among whom they lived, in whose sight I made myself known to them so as to bring them out of the land of Egypt.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The repeated phrase "for the sake of my name" (lema'an shemi) is the governing theological motif of Ezekiel 20 (cf. vv. 14, 22, 44). YHWH's restraint of judgment is grounded not in Israel's worthiness but in the protection of his own reputation among the nations. This is consistent with Ezekiel's pervasive concern for divine glory. (Ezek 36:22; Isa 48:9-11)

verses.flow: And I gave them my statutes and caused them to know my ordinances, which, if a man does them, he shall live by them.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The phrase "if a man does them, he shall live by them" (Lev 18:5) is directly cited and becomes a recurring refrain (vv. 13, 21). Paul cites this same text in Gal 3:12 to contrast law and faith. Ezekiel's rehearsal of this formula in the context of repeated failure anticipates the need for a new covenant and a new heart. (Lev 18:5; Gal 3:12; Ezek 36:26-27)

verses.flow: Moreover, I gave them my Sabbaths, as a sign between me and them, that they might know that I am YHWH who sanctifies them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Sabbath is here defined as a "sign" (ot, H226) between YHWH and Israel — a covenant marker identical in language to the rainbow (Gen 9:13) and circumcision (Gen 17:11). The Sabbath functions not merely as rest but as a sanctification declaration: "I am YHWH who sanctifies them" (mekaddishem). Its profanation is therefore a covenantal rupture. (Exod 31:13-17; Gen 17:11)

verses.flow: Nevertheless, my eye spared them, so that I did not destroy them, and I did not make a full end of them in the wilderness.
verses.note: HEBREW: "My eye spared them" uses the idiom tachos ayin (H2347), literally "my eye had pity." This anthropomorphic expression of divine compassion restraining deserved wrath is characteristic of Ezekiel (cf. 7:4, 9; 8:18; 9:10). The tension between justice and mercy is unresolved here by anything other than divine prerogative. (Ezek 9:5-6; Lam 2:17)

verses.flow: Moreover, I lifted up my hand to them in the wilderness, to scatter them among the nations and disperse them through the lands,
because they had not done my ordinances, but had rejected my statutes, and had profaned my Sabbaths, and their eyes were after their fathers' idols.
Moreover, I also gave them statutes that were not good and ordinances by which they could not live,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the most theologically difficult verses in Ezekiel — YHWH says he gave Israel "statutes that were not good." Most interpreters understand this as referring to divine abandonment: YHWH gave them over to the corrupted religious practices they chose (cf. Rom 1:24-28), perhaps referencing the laws of Canaan or distorted cultic ordinances. The judicial hardening motif (cf. Isa 6:9-10) is likely in view. Some see an allusion to Exod 22:29 misapplied to child sacrifice. (Rom 1:24; Isa 6:9; Ps 81:12)

verses.flow: and I defiled them through their very gifts — in their offering up all their firstborn — that I might devastate them, so that they might know that I am YHWH.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Offering up all their firstborn" refers to the Molech cult practice of child sacrifice, attested archaeologically in the Tophet at Hinnom and at Carthaginian tophets. Ezekiel uses "defile" (tame, H2930) — the same root used for ritual impurity — indicating these acts rendered Israel cultically unclean. This verse shockingly attributes the practice to divine judicial hardening. (Jer 7:31; Lev 18:21; Deut 12:31)

verses.flow: And I said to them: What is the high place (bamah) to which you are going? So its name is called Bamah to this day.
verses.note: HEBREW: Verse 29 contains a wordplay on bamah (H1116, "high place") and the rhetorical question "What (mah) is the high place (bamah) to which you go (ba'im)?" — the etymology may be a folk etymology connecting ba (come) + mah (what), mocking the institution. High places were Canaanite cultic sites adopted by Israel, condemned throughout the Deuteronomistic literature. (1 Kgs 3:2; 2 Kgs 17:9-11; Num 33:52)

verses.flow: And what comes up in your spirit shall never come to pass — when you say: We will be like the nations, like the tribes of the lands, serving wood and stone.
"As I live," declares the Lord YHWH, "surely with a mighty hand and with an outstretched arm and with wrath poured out, I will be king over you.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "I will be king over you" (amloch aleichem) with the threefold "mighty hand, outstretched arm, wrath poured out" mirrors Exodus language (Deut 4:34; 5:15) but now applied to a new exodus — YHWH reasserting his kingship over a rebellious vassals. This is the language of the divine sovereign reclaiming his realm, consistent with the divine council theology of YHWH as cosmic king. (Deut 4:34; Mic 4:6-7; Rev 19:6)

verses.flow: And I will bring you into the wilderness of the peoples, and there I will enter into judgment with you face to face.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "wilderness of the peoples" as the site of a new covenant judgment and purification parallels the Sinai wilderness of the first Exodus. This new wilderness period anticipates the eschatological new exodus, a theme central to Ezekiel 34-37 and to the NT's understanding of Christ leading his people through trial into inheritance. (Hos 2:14-15; Ezek 34:11-16; Rev 12:14)

verses.flow: "And I will cause you to pass under the rod, and I will bring you into the bond of the covenant.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Cause you to pass under the rod" (abar tachat hashebet) is an image from shepherding: the shepherd's rod counted and inspected sheep passing into the fold (cf. Lev 27:32). This judgment is not annihilation but examination and enrollment — separating the faithful remnant from rebels. The "bond of the covenant" (masoret habrit) reflects the binding, obligatory nature of covenantal relationship. (Lev 27:32; Jer 33:13; Ezek 34:17)

verses.flow: For on my holy mountain, on the mountain height of Israel," declares the Lord YHWH, "there all the house of Israel, all of them, shall serve me in the land; there I will accept them, and there I will require your contributions and the choicest of your gifts with all your holy things.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "My holy mountain, the mountain height of Israel" anticipates the restored worship on Zion described in chapters 40-48. YHWH's acceptance of Israel's offerings uses the vocabulary of Levitical sacrifice but transforms it eschatologically. This passage undergirds the NT vision of the gathered church as YHWH's restored people offering spiritual worship. (Isa 56:7; Ezek 43:7; Rom 12:1)

verses.flow: As a pleasing aroma I will accept you, when I bring you out from the peoples and gather you from the lands where you have been scattered; and I will manifest myself as holy among you in the sight of the nations.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "As a pleasing aroma (reach nichoach) I will accept you" applies to the restored Israel the same language used of acceptable burnt offerings (Gen 8:21; Lev 1:9). This is typologically significant: the nation itself becomes the offering. Paul employs this exact sacrificial language for Christ (Eph 5:2) and for the Gentile mission (Rom 15:16), suggesting the eschatological ingathering fulfills this Ezekielian promise. (Gen 8:21; Eph 5:2; Rom 15:16)

verses.flow: And you shall know that I am YHWH, when I deal with you for my name's sake — not according to your evil ways, nor according to your corrupt deeds, O house of Israel," declares the Lord YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chapter closes with the ultimate theological inversion: YHWH's dealing with Israel "for my name's sake — not according to your evil ways" is pure grace. This anticipates the new covenant of Ezekiel 36 where YHWH acts unilaterally to restore, cleanse, and transform his people. It is the theological bedrock upon which the NT gospel rests. (Ezek 36:22-32; Titus 3:5; Eph 2:8-9)

verses.flow: And say to the land of Israel, Thus says YHWH: Behold, I am against you, and I will draw out my sword from its sheath and cut off from you both the righteous and the wicked.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The cutting off of "both the righteous and the wicked" (Hebrew: tzaddiq and rasha) in indiscriminate judgment reflects the totality of covenantal wrath when a nation is given over to destruction (cf. Gen 18:23-25). This parallels Ezekiel's broader theme of communal accountability, though he elsewhere stresses individual responsibility (ch. 18). The sword as the instrument of divine judgment is a recurring motif in Ezekiel (Ezek 5:2, 17; 6:3).

verses.flow: And it shall be, when they say to you, "Why are you groaning?" that you shall say, "Because of the report — for it comes, and every heart shall melt, and all hands shall go limp, and every spirit shall faint, and all knees shall drip with water. Behold, it comes and it shall be done," declares the Lord YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "every spirit shall faint" uses the Hebrew ruach (H7307, spirit/breath/wind), signifying the collapse of inner resolve. The expression "knees shall drip with water" is a vivid Hebrew idiom for extreme terror, likely referring to involuntary bodily response. The double declaration "it comes and it shall be done" emphasizes the irrevocability of divine decree.

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying,
"Son of man, prophesy and say: Thus says YHWH: Say, A sword, a sword — it is sharpened and also polished!
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The "sword song" of verses 9-17 is a Hebrew poem with its own distinctive meter and refrain, set apart from the surrounding prose oracles. The repetition "a sword, a sword" (Hebrew: cherev, cherev, H2719) functions as an anaphora of judgment, paralleling ancient Near Eastern war poetry. This song may have been a known dirge form that Ezekiel adapted for prophetic use.

verses.flow: Sharpened to make a slaughter, polished to flash like lightning! Or shall we rejoice? The scepter of my son despises every tree.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The phrase "the scepter of my son despises every tree" is interpretively contested. The Hebrew reads: shevet beni meazet kol-ets. "My son" (beni) likely refers to Israel (cf. Exod 4:22), and the scepter of royalty — the Davidic line — is here being brought low. This creates an ironic contrast with messianic scepter passages (Gen 49:10; Num 24:17), suggesting that the current Davidic king (Zedekiah) has forfeited the scepter's dignity.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "testing" (Hebrew: bachan, H974 — to test/assay as metal) implies that the sword-judgment functions as a refiner's assay. The rhetorical question "what if even the scepter that despises shall be no more?" echoes the Davidic crisis: will the royal line survive? This tension is resolved only in verse 27 with the coming of the one whose right it is.
