verses.flow: "And you, son of man, appoint for yourself two roads by which the sword of the king of Babylon may come. From a single land the two shall come forth. And cut out a signpost; at the head of the road to the city cut it out.
You shall appoint a road for the sword to come to Rabbah of the Ammonites, and to Judah, to Jerusalem the fortified.

For the king of Babylon shall stand at the parting of the road, at the head of the two roads, to practice divination: he shall shake the arrows, he shall consult the household idols (teraphim), he shall inspect the liver.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The divination practices enumerated here — arrow-shaking (rhabdomancy), consulting teraphim (household gods), and inspecting the liver (hepatoscopy) — are all well-attested in ancient Mesopotamian and Canaanite ritual practice. Cuneiform liver models used for haruspicy have been discovered at Mari and Ugarit, corroborating the historical accuracy of this account. The teraphim (H8655) are the same objects found in Laban's household (Gen 31:19) and in Micah's shrine (Judg 17-18).

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Into his right hand has come the divination for Jerusalem — to set battering rams, to open the mouth for slaughter, to lift up the voice with a war cry, to set battering rams against the gates, to heap up siege mounds, to build a siege wall.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The siege technology described — battering rams (khar, H3733), siege ramps (solelah, H5550), and palisades — matches what is known from Assyrian and Babylonian military reliefs, particularly those from Sennacherib's campaigns at Lachish. Nebuchadnezzar's siege of Jerusalem (588-586 BC) used precisely these methods, as confirmed by both the Babylonian Chronicles and Lachish Letter archaeology.

verses.flow: Therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: Because you have caused your iniquity to be remembered, in that your transgressions are uncovered, so that in all your deeds your sins appear — because you have come to remembrance, you shall be captured by the hand.
And you, O slain wicked one, prince of Israel, whose day has come — at the time of the final iniquity —
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The address to the "slain wicked one, prince of Israel" is directed at Zedekiah, the last reigning Davidic king before the exile. The phrase "whose day has come at the time of the final iniquity" (Hebrew: et avon qetz) signals that this is an eschatological reckoning, not merely a historical punishment. The "end" (qetz, H7093) of iniquity language echoes the apocalyptic framework of Ezekiel's contemporary Daniel (Dan 8:17, 19; 9:26).

verses.flow: Thus says the Lord YHWH: Remove the turban and take off the crown. This shall not be as it was. Exalt the low, and bring low the exalted.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The command to remove the mitznefet (H4701, turban — the high-priestly headgear) and the atara (H5850, crown — the royal diadem) signals the collapse of both priestly and royal offices simultaneously, the twin pillars of Israel's theocratic governance. The reversal formula "exalt the low, bring low the exalted" (Hebrew: hashpilah hagavohah, hagbehah hashephalah) anticipates Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:52) and is the covenantal inversion principle rooted in the Abrahamic blessing/curse structure.

verses.flow: A ruin, a ruin, a ruin — I will make it. Even this shall not be, until he comes whose right it is, and to him I will give it.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The triple repetition "a ruin, a ruin, a ruin" (Hebrew: avvah avvah avvah, H5754) is emphatic superlative, indicating utter and total overturning. The climactic promise "until he comes whose right it is" (Hebrew: ad asher-yavo asher-lo hammishpat) is widely regarded as a direct echo of Genesis 49:10 ("until Shiloh comes" or "until he comes to whom it belongs"). This is a messianic crux — the Davidic throne, though overturned, awaits its true and rightful heir. Early Jewish targums and Christian interpreters from Origen onward have read this as pointing to the Messiah. The NT fulfillment is in the enthronement of Jesus as the Son of David (Luke 1:32-33; Rev 19:16).

verses.flow: And you, son of man, prophesy and say: Thus says the Lord YHWH concerning the Ammonites and concerning their reproach. Say: A sword, a sword — drawn for slaughter, polished to consume, to flash like lightning —
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The turn to the Ammonites in verse 28 shows that divine judgment operates on a trans-national, cosmic scale — YHWH's sovereignty over Babylon does not exempt neighboring nations from reckoning. The repetition of the sword-poem imagery (cf. v. 9) applied now to Ammon suggests a structural parallelism: the same instrument of divine wrath strikes both the covenant people and the pagan nations. This reflects the divine council's governance over all nations (Deut 32:8-9; Ps 82).

verses.flow: You shall be fuel for the fire; your blood shall be in the midst of the land. You shall be remembered no more, for I, YHWH, have spoken."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The declaration "you shall be remembered no more" (Hebrew: lo tizzakeri, from zakar H2142) is a formula of complete annihilation from historical and covenantal memory. In Hebrew thought, to be "remembered" by God is to exist and to be sustained; to be "not remembered" is to be utterly blotted out (cf. Ps 109:15; Exod 17:14). The Ammonites' extinction as a people is thereby sealed — a stark contrast to the remnant promises extended to Israel even within the same judgment oracles.

verses.flow: By your blood that you have shed you have become guilty, and by your idols that you have made you have defiled yourself; and you have brought your days near, and have come to your years of judgment. Therefore I have made you a reproach to the nations, and a mockery to all the lands.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The connection between bloodshed, idolatry, and defilement reflects the covenantal logic of Leviticus 18-20 — the land itself vomits out its inhabitants when polluted by these sins (Lev 18:25,28). The phrase "brought your days near" indicates that judgment is not arbitrary but the culmination of accumulated covenantal unfaithfulness. (Lev 18:25), (Deut 28:37)

verses.flow: Behold, the princes of Israel, each according to his arm, have been in you for the purpose of shedding blood.
Father and mother they have treated lightly in you; the sojourner they have oppressed in your midst; the fatherless and the widow they have wronged in you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The triad of "sojourner, fatherless, and widow" is the classic covenantal vulnerable class whose protection YHWH demands (Exod 22:21-22; Deut 10:18; Zech 7:10). Their mistreatment is not merely social failure but direct covenant violation. The dishonoring of parents (Exod 20:12; Deut 27:16) appears first, framing the social collapse as rooted in broken vertical relationships. (Exod 22:21), (Deut 10:18)

verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Eating upon the mountains" refers to participation in the high-place (bamah) cult meals associated with pagan worship (cf. Ezek 18:6, 11, 15), a practice condemned throughout the prophets. These sacred meals often accompanied sexual rites and idol feasts in Canaanite religion, attested in Ugaritic texts. THEOLOGICAL: The clustering of social sins with cultic sins illustrates the indivisibility of the two tables of the law in prophetic ethics.

verses.flow: In you they have taken bribes for the purpose of shedding blood; interest (neshek) and increase (tarbit) you have taken, and you have gained by extortion from your neighbors, and you have forgotten me, declares the Lord YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Neshek" (Strong's H5392) literally means "bite," referring to interest charged on loans, and "tarbit" (Strong's H8636) means "increase" or "surplus profit." Together they form a hendiadys for usurious exploitation forbidden in Torah (Lev 25:36-37; Deut 23:19). THEOLOGICAL: The climax of the sin list — "you have forgotten me" — reveals that all social and economic injustice is at root a theological failure: the forgetting of YHWH as the true owner of land, wealth, and persons.

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying,
"Son of man, the house of Israel has become dross (sigim) to me; all of them are bronze and tin and iron and lead in the midst of the furnace (kur); they have become the dross of silver.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Sigim" (Strong's H5509, dross) refers to the waste material separated from silver during smelting — worthless impurity. The metaphor of the smelting furnace (kur, Strong's H3564) appears also in Deut 4:20 and 1 Kgs 8:51 where Egypt is called "the iron furnace," providing an exodus typology. TYPOLOGY: As YHWH used Egypt's furnace to forge Israel as a people, so now Jerusalem becomes a furnace — but this time producing only dross, inverting the exodus narrative. (Deut 4:20), (1 Kgs 8:51)

verses.flow: There is a conspiracy (qesher) of her prophets in her midst, like a roaring lion tearing the prey; they have devoured souls (nefashot); they have taken treasure and precious things; they have made many widows in her midst.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The use of "conspiracy" (qesher, Strong's H7195) for the prophets is striking — it implies organized, deliberate collusion in deception rather than mere individual error. The lion imagery (cf. Ezek 19:3,6) applied to false prophets inverts their role: instead of being undershepherds they are predators of the flock. DIVINE_COUNCIL: False prophecy in the OT is connected to spirits of deception operating in the divine council — a lying spirit permitted to mislead (1 Kgs 22:19-23), giving the false prophet's words a cosmic, not merely human, dimension. (1 Kgs 22:19-23), (Jer 23:14)

verses.flow: Her priests have done violence to my Torah (torat) and have profaned my holy things; between the holy and the common they have made no distinction, and between the unclean and the clean they have not taught, and from my sabbaths they have hidden their eyes, and I am profaned among them.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Torat" here is the priestly Torah — the instruction (Strong's H8451) entrusted to priests to distinguish clean and unclean, holy and common (Lev 10:10-11; Mal 2:7-8). The verb "chamas" (violence, Strong's H2555) applied to priestly handling of Torah is a devastating indictment: they have not merely neglected it but done violence to it. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is one of the most concentrated indictments of the priesthood in all the prophets, showing that the corruption is systemic across all leadership classes. (Lev 10:10), (Mal 2:7-8)

verses.flow: And I sought from them a man among them who would build up the wall and stand in the breach before me on behalf of the land, so that I should not destroy it — but I found none.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "man standing in the breach" is one of the most poignant images of intercessory mediation in the OT (cf. Ps 106:23 where Moses stood in the breach). YHWH actively seeks a human intercessor — revealing his relational nature and his desire to show mercy rather than destroy. The failure to find one does not indicate divine fatalism but deepens the indictment against the entire leadership class. TYPOLOGY: The Mediator who ultimately stands in the breach between holy God and sinful humanity is the Messiah Yeshua, who makes intercession for transgressors (Isa 53:12; Heb 7:25). (Ps 106:23), (Isa 53:12), (Heb 7:25)

verses.note: HEBREW: "Son of man" (ben-adam, H1121 + H120) — a standard address to Ezekiel emphasizing his creaturely humanity before the divine. The phrase grounds the prophet's identity in mortality even as he receives transcendent visions. Used over 90 times in Ezekiel, it is unparalleled in this frequency among the prophets.

verses.flow: and they played the harlot in Egypt; in their youth they played the harlot. There their breasts were pressed, and there the nipples of their virginity were handled.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The allegory begins in Egypt, the paradigmatic place of Israel's origins and, simultaneously, of its first spiritual adultery. Archaeological and textual evidence from the New Kingdom period confirms widespread syncretistic religious practices in the Nile Delta region where the Israelites dwelt. Egypt here functions typologically as the source of the harlotry that would characterize both kingdoms throughout their histories.

verses.flow: And their names were: Oholah the elder, and Oholibah her sister. And they became mine, and they bore sons and daughters. And as for their names: Samaria is Oholah, and Jerusalem is Oholibah.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Oholah" ('oholah, H168) means "her tent," and "Oholibah" ('oholiyvah) means "my tent is in her." The name distinction is theologically charged: Oholah (Samaria) has her own tent — a counterfeit worship center — while Oholibah (Jerusalem) is the city where YHWH has placed his own dwelling (mishkan). Jerusalem's greater guilt is thus encoded in her very name, since she sinned despite having YHWH's true sanctuary in her midst. (1 Kgs 12:28-29; Ps 78:60)

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "she became a byword (shem) among women" uses shem (H8034, "name/reputation") to indicate that Samaria's fall became proverbial. verses.note: HISTORICAL: The portrayal of Babylonian warriors "in vermilion" (shashah, reddish pigment) on walls reflects actual Babylonian artistic practice. Procession Street and the Ishtar Gate in Babylon featured vivid polychrome glazed reliefs of warriors and divine beings. Ezekiel's audience would have recognized this cultural detail as authentic, lending visceral force to the accusation that Jerusalem was seduced by Babylonian imperial imagery itself.

The irony is sharp: Jerusalem sought Babylon as a lover, was defiled, then grew disgusted with Babylon — yet YHWH's disgust with Jerusalem for the very act of this seeking is the operative judgment. Infidelity ultimately produces only desolation.

verses.note: HEBREW: "Paramours" (piylagshim, H6370, often rendered "concubines") here carries the force of illicit lovers. The explicit anatomical comparisons to donkeys and horses (reflecting hypersexualized imagery of the ancient Near East, also found in Egyptian and Mesopotamian texts) are deliberately shocking. Ezekiel employs graphic language not for prurience but to confront the people with the ugliness of their spiritual condition without euphemism.

verses.flow: Therefore, O Oholibah, thus says the Lord YHWH: Behold, I will stir up your lovers against you, from whom your soul has turned in disgust, and I will bring them against you from every side:
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The formula "thus says the Lord YHWH" (koh amar Adonai YHWH) occurs repeatedly in Ezekiel as a messenger formula modeled on the royal herald announcing decrees from the divine council chamber. YHWH's sovereign authority over Babylon and Assyria — the very empires Jerusalem courted — is here invoked to show that those nations are instruments in the hands of the true cosmic King. (Isa 10:5; Hab 1:6)

verses.flow: the sons of Babylon and all the Chaldeans, Pekod and Shoa and Koa, and all the sons of Assyria with them — desirable young men, governors and commanders all of them, officers and men of renown, all of them riding on horses.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Pekod, Shoa, and Koa are Aramaic and Babylonian tribal groups in Mesopotamia. Pekod (Puqudu) is attested in Akkadian sources as a tribe east of the Tigris; Shoa (Sutu) and Koa (Qutu) are similarly documented in Neo-Babylonian administrative texts. Ezekiel's specificity of tribal names demonstrates firsthand knowledge of Babylonian geo-political realities consistent with his exile context.

verses.flow: And I will set my jealousy against you, and they shall deal with you in fury. They shall cut off your nose and your ears, and your survivors shall fall by the sword. They shall take your sons and your daughters, and your survivors shall be devoured by fire.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The mutilation of nose and ears was a documented punishment for adultery in the ancient Near East, attested in Middle Assyrian Law codes (MAL A §4-5). Ezekiel employs this legal practice as the literal-judicial consequence of Jerusalem's metaphorical adultery, connecting the allegory to recognizable juridical norms of the ancient world and demonstrating that YHWH's judgment operates within intelligible frameworks of justice.

verses.flow: In the way of your sister you have walked; therefore I will give her cup into your hand.
Thus says the Lord YHWH: You shall drink your sister's cup, which is deep and wide; you shall be laughed to scorn and held in derision — it holds much.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "cup" of divine wrath is a recurring prophetic motif that reaches its ultimate typological fulfillment in the New Testament's portrayal of Christ drinking the cup of God's wrath in Gethsemane (Matt 26:39) and in the cup of Babylon's judgment in Revelation (Rev 14:10; 16:19; 18:6). Jerusalem drinks the cup poured out by YHWH; the Messiah drinks the cup on behalf of his people. (Isa 51:17, 22; Jer 25:15)

The result is that the sinner must "bear" (nasa, H5375) her own iniquity — the forensic weight of guilt returns to its owner when covenant relationship is severed.

verses.flow: For they have committed adultery, and blood is on their hands. With their idols they have committed adultery, and even their children whom they bore to me they have offered up to them as food.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The charge of offering children "born to me" to idols connects to the Molech cult (Lev 18:21; 20:2-5) and represents the ultimate inversion of covenant: children who belonged to YHWH as covenant members of his people were sacrificed to demonic powers. This constitutes the gravest possible act of covenant treachery and explains the severity of the judgment. (Ps 106:37-38)

verses.flow: Moreover, this they have done to me: they have defiled my sanctuary on the same day and profaned my sabbaths.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The double profanation — sanctuary and sabbath defiled on the same day — represents the violation of both sacred space and sacred time, the two primary structures through which YHWH's holiness (qedoshim) was to be honored in Israel's cultic life. The simultaneous violation intensifies the offense, suggesting a studied, deliberate desecration rather than inadvertent error.

The incense (qetoreth, H7004) and oil (shemen, H8081) belonged to YHWH by cultic designation (Exod 30:22-38); using them in the service of foreign lovers constitutes sacrilege of a precise and calculated kind, converting consecrated elements into instruments of idolatrous seduction.

verses.flow: But righteous men shall judge them with the judgment of adulteresses, and with the judgment of women who shed blood, because they are adulteresses, and blood is on their hands.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Righteous men shall judge them" — the juridical language here anticipates the divine assembly rendering verdict. Some interpreters connect this to the divine council motif in which heavenly beings participate in executing YHWH's decrees (Ps 82:1; Dan 7:10). The judgment of adultery and bloodshed carries the death penalty under Torah (Lev 20:10; Num 35:16), confirming that what follows is the execution of established covenantal law.

verses.flow: Thus I will put an end to lewdness in the land, that all women may be warned and not commit lewdness as you have done.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The purpose clause — "that all women may be warned" — reveals that Israel's judgment has a didactic, universal function. The nation's catastrophic end is intended to serve as a warning (musar, H4148 — "discipline, instruction") to the nations. This redemptive-corrective purpose within judgment is characteristic of Ezekiel's theology: even wrath serves YHWH's ultimately instructive and sovereign purposes. (Rom 15:4)

verses.flow: And they shall return your lewdness upon you, and you shall bear the penalty of your sinful idolatry, and you shall know that I am the Lord YHWH."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chapter closes with the covenant recognition formula: "you shall know that I am the Lord YHWH" (yada, H3045). This formula, occurring over 70 times in Ezekiel, is the ultimate goal of both judgment and restoration — that all parties come to experiential, covenantal knowledge of who YHWH truly is. The doubling of "Lord YHWH" (Adonai YHWH) at the chapter's close is emphatic, reasserting divine sovereignty over the entire tragic narrative. (Ezek 6:7; 36:23)

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me in the ninth year, in the tenth month, on the tenth day of the month, saying:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The date given — ninth year, tenth month, tenth day — corresponds precisely to January 15, 588 BCE by modern reckoning, the day Nebuchadnezzar began his siege of Jerusalem (cf. 2 Kings 25:1; Jer 52:4). That Ezekiel, in Babylonian exile, received this revelation on the exact day of the siege's commencement without human communication underscores the supernatural nature of prophetic knowledge. This date was later commemorated as a fast day in Jewish tradition (Zechariah 8:19).

verses.flow: "Son of man, write down for yourself the name of this day, this very day — the king of Babylon has drawn near to Jerusalem this very day.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The command to "write down the name of this day" (Hebrew: katav leka et-shem hayyom) transforms the date into a legal witness and covenant document. YHWH's foreknowledge of the siege's commencement — and the demand that it be recorded — functions as proof of divine sovereignty over history. The phrase "this very day" (be-etzem hayyom hazzeh) appears also at key covenantal moments (Gen 7:13; Exod 12:17), marking divine action of decisive significance.

verses.flow: And speak a parable to the house of rebellion, and say to them: Thus says the Lord YHWH: Set on the pot, set it on, and also pour water into it.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "house of rebellion" (bet hameri, H4805) is one of Ezekiel's characteristic designations for Israel, used repeatedly throughout the book (Ezek 2:5–8; 3:9; 12:2–3). The "parable" (mashal, H4912) here functions as an enacted prophetic metaphor — the cooking pot image draws on imagery previously used in Ezek 11:3–11, where Jerusalem's wicked leaders ironically called themselves "the meat" protected by the pot-city. YHWH now turns the irony against them.

verses.flow: Therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: Woe to the city of blood, to the pot in which there is scum (chel'ah), and whose scum has not gone out of it! By piece, by piece bring it out — no lot has fallen upon it.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word "scum" or "rust" (chel'ah, H2457) is a rare term appearing only in this chapter and denoting corrosion or encrusted filth on metal. It functions as a vivid metaphor for the accumulated moral corruption and bloodguilt within Jerusalem. The phrase "no lot has fallen upon it" (lo-naphal goral aleha) likely indicates that no merciful selection spares any piece — all are brought to judgment without the random mercy of a lot-cast.

verses.flow: For her blood is in her midst; upon a bare rock she has set it; she has not poured it upon the ground to cover it with dust.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: In Mosaic law, blood poured on the ground was to be covered with dust (Lev 17:13), symbolically returning life to the earth and acknowledging YHWH's ownership of blood. Jerusalem's brazen exposure of blood on bare rock — uncovered — represents defiant covenant violation and an accumulating cry for divine vengeance (cf. Gen 4:10; Job 16:18). The exposed blood becomes its own witness demanding retribution.

verses.flow: And set it empty upon its coals, that it may become hot and its bronze may burn, and its uncleanness be melted in it, its scum consumed.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The imagery of the pot set empty on coals until its bronze burns away speaks to the complete and thoroughgoing nature of divine purification-judgment. In ANE thought, bronze was associated with divine thrones and heavenly structures (cf. Ezek 1:7; Dan 10:6). The burning away of the bronze scum may carry resonances of YHWH's refining judgment that operates cosmically as well as historically, purging what is corrupted from his ordered creation.

verses.flow: I, YHWH, have spoken; it shall come and I will act. I will not relent, nor will I spare, nor will I be sorry. According to your ways and according to your deeds they shall judge you, declares the Lord YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The triple declaration "I will not relent (lo-afrug), nor spare (lo-achus), nor be sorry (lo-einachem)" constitutes one of Ezekiel's most emphatic statements of irreversible divine judgment. The language echoes YHWH's judicial role in covenant enforcement (cf. Deut 13:8; 19:13). Notably, judgment here is framed as judicial — "according to your ways and deeds they shall judge you" — suggesting the covenant court has rendered its final verdict. (Rev 20:12)

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying:
"Son of man, behold, I am taking away from you the desire of your eyes with a blow; yet you shall not mourn or weep, and your tears shall not flow.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "desire of your eyes" (machmad einekha, H4261) refers to Ezekiel's wife, and the same phrase recurs in verse 21 for the Temple itself, creating a deliberate theological parallel. The prophet's personal bereavement becomes a living parable of YHWH's own grief and the community's coming loss of the sanctuary. This is one of the most emotionally profound prophetic sign-acts in all of Scripture, where the prophet's private suffering becomes the vessel for divine revelation.

verses.flow: Groan silently; make no mourning for the dead. Bind your turban upon yourself, and put your sandals on your feet; do not cover your lips, and do not eat the bread of men.
verses.note: HEBREW: The instruction to "groan silently" (he-anek dom, lit. "sigh, be silent") combines two forms of grief suppression in one phrase. Normal mourning rites in ancient Israel included uncovering the head, removing sandals, covering the upper lip, and receiving communal "bread of men" (lechem anashim) — a technical term for the funeral meal brought by neighbors (cf. Jer 16:7; Hos 9:4). Ezekiel's prohibition of all these constitutes a total inversion of the mourning ritual, itself a sign of judgment so extreme that normal grief rites are inadequate.

verses.flow: And I said to them: The word of YHWH came to me, saying:

"Say to the house of Israel: Thus says the Lord YHWH: Behold, I will profane my sanctuary, the pride of your strength, the desire of your eyes, and the compassion of your soul — and your sons and your daughters whom you have left behind shall fall by the sword.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The profaning of the sanctuary (miqdash, H4720) — called "the pride of your strength, the desire of your eyes, and the compassion of your soul" — signals the termination of the Mosaic covenant's cultic center. This language anticipates Christ's announcement of the Temple's destruction (Matt 24:1–2) and the theological shift from the Jerusalem Temple to Christ himself as the new locus of divine presence (John 2:19–21; Heb 9:11). The exile of divine glory from a defiled sanctuary is a recurring pattern in redemptive history.

verses.flow: And Ezekiel shall be to you a sign (mopheth); according to all that he has done you shall do. When it comes, then you shall know that I am the Lord YHWH."
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "sign" (mopheth, H4159) denotes a portent or wonder — an event or person that prefigures and embodies a divine message. Ezekiel is declared to be not merely a speaker of signs but a living embodiment of one. The same term is used of Moses and Aaron's wonders before Pharaoh (Exod 7:3; 11:9). In Ezekiel's case, his whole manner of life — silenced, grieving, performing strange acts — becomes prophetic testimony to Israel's coming fate. (Luke 2:34)

verses.flow: "And you, son of man — shall it not be on the day when I take from them their stronghold, their joyful glory, the desire of their eyes, and the lifting up of their soul — their sons and their daughters:
On that day, a fugitive will come to you to cause your ears to hear.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The announcement of a coming "fugitive" (pelitah, one who has escaped, H6412) who will bring news of Jerusalem's fall forms an important structural hinge in the book. The fulfillment is recorded in Ezek 33:21–22, where a fugitive arrives on the fifth month of the twelfth year. During the interval between this prophecy and that arrival, Ezekiel is bound in prophetic silence (cf. Ezek 3:26–27; 33:22), making the sign-act of his muteness a sustained, years-long enacted prophecy.

verses.flow: On that day your mouth will be opened to the one who has fled, and you shall speak and be no longer mute. And you shall be a sign (mopheth) to them, and they shall know that I am YHWH."
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This verse forms a literary bracket with Ezek 3:26–27, where Ezekiel's mouth was shut and he was made mute except when YHWH granted him speech. The restoration of his voice upon receiving news of Jerusalem's fall signals the transition from judgment oracles (chapters 1–24) to oracles of restoration (chapters 33–48). The repeated phrase "you shall be a sign (mopheth) to them" inclusio-brackets this section of the book, emphasizing Ezekiel's total prophetic identification with his message. (Ezek 33:21–22)

verses.flow: And say to the sons of Ammon, Hear the word of the Lord YHWH: Thus says the Lord YHWH, Because you said 'Aha!' over my sanctuary, when it was profaned, and over the land of Israel, when it was made desolate, and over the house of Judah, when they went into exile,
verses.note: HEBREW: The interjection "Aha!" (Hebrew: he'ach, Strong's H1889) is an exclamation of malicious glee or schadenfreude, expressing contemptuous satisfaction at another's misfortune. The Ammonites rejoiced at three successive catastrophes: the profaning of the sanctuary (586 BC), the desolation of the land, and the exile of Judah, revealing their deep hostility toward the covenant people. The phrase "my sanctuary" (miqdashi) on YHWH's lips underscores that the temple's destruction was not mere geopolitical loss but a desecration of the divine dwelling. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's judgment on Ammon is grounded in their response to his redemptive purposes — rejoicing at covenant judgment rather than mourning constitutes a unique category of guilt (cf. Obad 12-14).

verses.flow: And I will make Rabbah a pasture for camels, and the sons of Ammon a resting place for flocks; and you shall know that I am YHWH.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Rabbah (modern Amman, Jordan) was the capital of Ammon, situated on the upper Jabbok River. The transformation of a royal city into pastureland for camels represents the complete overthrow of urban civilization and the return of the land to nomadic use, a fate attested in ancient Near Eastern curse formulae. The "sons of the east" (bene-qedem) likely refers to Arabian tribal confederacies who historically pressed into the Transjordanian region following the collapse of Assyrian and then Babylonian hegemony.

verses.flow: therefore, behold, I have stretched out my hand against you, and I will give you as plunder to the nations; and I will cut you off from the peoples and cause you to perish from the lands; I will destroy you. And you shall know that I am YHWH.
Thus says the Lord YHWH: Because Moab and Seir said, Behold, the house of Judah is like all the nations,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Moab's offense is distinctly theological — to say "the house of Judah is like all the nations" is to deny the elect status of Israel and the faithfulness (hesed, Strong's H2617) of YHWH to his covenant. This leveling claim strikes at the heart of covenantal particularism: if Judah's fall proves she is merely one nation among others, then YHWH is merely one deity among others. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The inclusion of "Seir" (Edom) here is textually notable; some scholars see a double oracle compressed, while the joining of Moab and Edom reflects their geographic and theological kinship as descendants of Lot and Esau respectively, both outside the Abrahamic covenant line of promise.

verses.flow: therefore, behold, I am opening the flank of Moab from the cities, from its cities on its frontier, the glory of the land — Beth-jeshimoth, Baal-meon, and Kiriathaim —
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Beth-jeshimoth, Baal-meon, and Kiriathaim were frontier cities on the northern and eastern edges of Moab, representing the vulnerable "flank" (kateph, shoulder) of the nation. Their mention signals that the judgment begins at the border and works inward. Archaeological surveys of the Moabite plateau confirm dense settlement in these regions during the Iron Age II period, consistent with their status as prized frontier cities.

verses.flow: Thus says the Lord YHWH: Because Edom acted with taking of vengeance against the house of Judah, and they have incurred great guilt by taking vengeance upon them,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Edom's guilt is specified as "acting with vengeance" (naqam, Strong's H5358) against Judah, compounding the offense by the phrase "and they have incurred great guilt" (va'yashmem asham, they have made themselves guilty of guilt). The doubling of guilt language intensifies the charge. Edom's behavior at the fall of Jerusalem is corroborated by Psalm 137:7, Obadiah 10-14, and Lamentations 4:21-22, forming a consistent biblical testimony of Edomite treachery. TYPOLOGY: Edom functions throughout prophetic literature as a type of persistent covenantal hostility, and its ultimate judgment (cf. Isa 34, 63; Amos 9:12) anticipates eschatological themes of the vindication of God's people (cf. Rom 9:13).

verses.flow: And I will lay my vengeance upon Edom by the hand of my people Israel; and they shall act in Edom according to my anger and according to my wrath; and they shall know my vengeance, declares the Lord YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "by the hand of my people Israel" is remarkable — YHWH delegates the execution of his vengeance against Edom to Israel as his instrument. The expression "my vengeance" (niqmati) occurring twice in this verse stresses that this is not Israel's private revenge but YHWH's own covenantal reckoning carried out through human agency. TYPOLOGY: This pattern of divine vengeance executed through the covenant people anticipates the Messiah's role as the divine warrior (cf. Rev 19:11-16), in whom YHWH's own vengeance and his people's deliverance converge.

verses.flow: therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: Behold, I am stretching out my hand against the Philistines, and I will cut off the Cherethites and destroy the remnant of the seacoast.
verses.note: HEBREW: The "Cherethites" (Kereti, Strong's H3774) are closely associated with the Philistines, likely denoting either a sub-group or a geographic designation connected with Crete (Caphtor). They appear elsewhere as elite warriors in David's personal guard (2 Sam 8:18; 15:18), suggesting their renown as mercenary soldiers. The term "seacoast" (chof hayam) identifies the Philistine homeland as the Mediterranean coastal plain, whose "remnant" YHWH promises to destroy utterly — language of total judgment (herem-like completeness). HISTORICAL: The Philistines had been a persistent thorn in Israel's side since the period of the Judges, and their historical enmity (enmity described as "perpetual," olam, everlasting) makes their judgment not merely circumstantial but the culmination of centuries of unrelenting opposition to YHWH's covenant people.

verses.flow: And I will execute great vengeance upon them with wrathful rebukes; and they shall know that I am YHWH, when I lay my vengeance upon them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The oracles against the nations in Ezekiel 25-32 all culminate in the recognition formula "and they shall know that I am YHWH" (ve'yadu ki-ani YHWH). This refrain, appearing over 70 times in Ezekiel, is the book's theological heartbeat: all history — whether judgment upon the pagan nations or discipline of Israel — is oriented toward the universal acknowledgment of YHWH's sovereign identity. STRUCTURE: Chapter 25 functions as an introductory panel to the larger oracles-against-the-nations corpus (chapters 25-32), targeting four peoples in four compass directions relative to Israel — Ammon (northeast), Moab (east), Edom (southeast), and Philistia (west) — forming a symbolic encirclement of the land under divine judgment.

verses.flow: And it came to pass in the eleventh year, on the first of the month, the word of YHWH came to me, saying,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The date formula "eleventh year, first of the month" is ambiguous in the Masoretic Text — the month is not specified, though many scholars suggest the first month (Nisan), placing this oracle shortly after Jerusalem's fall (586 BC). The LXX reads "first day of the first month," which some textual critics prefer. The oracle against Tyre follows the news of Jerusalem's destruction, which provides the dramatic occasion for Tyre's gloating response.

verses.flow: "Son of man, because Tyre has said against Jerusalem, 'Aha! She is broken, the gate of the peoples; she has turned to me; I will be filled, she has been laid waste!' —
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Tyre's sin is explicitly commercial and covetantal — she sees Jerusalem's fall as a trade opportunity, celebrating that the "gate of the peoples" has been broken so she might absorb the commerce that passed through Judah. The phrase "gate of the peoples" (delet ha-ammim) likely refers to Jerusalem's role as a trade hub and political center. This calculating greed in the face of covenant judgment is the specific transgression YHWH addresses.

verses.flow: therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: Behold, I am against you, O Tyre, and I will bring up against you many nations, as the sea brings up its waves.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The formula "thus says the Lord YHWH" (koh amar Adonai YHWH) occurs repeatedly in Ezekiel's oracles against the nations, functioning as a royal proclamation from the divine council chamber. YHWH as sovereign declares His executive decree against Tyre. The imagery of waves (gallim) echoes the ANE cosmological battle motif of the sea as a threatening, uncontrollable force — here YHWH uses this very imagery as His instrument of judgment.

For thus says the Lord YHWH: Behold, I am bringing against Tyre from the north Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon, king of kings, with horses and with chariots, and with horsemen, and a company and a great people.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Nebuchadrezzar" is the more accurate Hebrew transliteration (Nebukadretstsar) of the Akkadian Nabu-kudurri-usur than the more familiar "Nebuchadnezzar." The title "king of kings" (melek ha-melakhim) was a standard Babylonian royal epithet used by the Neo-Babylonian emperors. Historically, Nebuchadrezzar's siege of mainland Tyre lasted approximately 13 years (585–573 BC) but the island city remained largely intact — a tension the text itself later acknowledges (29:17–18).

verses.flow: With the hooves of his horses he will trample all your streets; your people he will slay by the sword, and your strong pillars (matstsevot) will fall to the ground.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Matstsevot" (Strong's H4676) refers to standing stones or pillars, often associated with sacred memorial sites, border markers, or cultic standing stones. In Phoenician contexts, such pillars had strong religious significance. Their falling symbolizes not merely military defeat but the collapse of Tyre's entire religious and civic order. The term may also carry an ironic echo of the sacred pillars Jachin and Boaz in Solomon's Temple (1 Kgs 7:15–22).

verses.flow: And they shall take up a lamentation (qinah) over you and say to you: How you have perished, O inhabited one from the seas, O city renowned, who was mighty on the sea, she and her inhabitants, who imposed her terror on all her inhabitants!
verses.note: HEBREW: "Qinah" (Strong's H7015) is the characteristic Hebrew term for a funeral lament or dirge, often composed in a distinctive 3+2 meter known as the qinah meter. This literary form ironically places the nations in the role of mourners at Tyre's funeral. The description of Tyre as "inhabited one from the seas" (yashevet yammim) reflects her unique identity as a dual city — both a coastal mainland settlement and the famous island fortress off the Levantine coast.

verses.flow: For thus says the Lord YHWH: When I make you a desolate city, like the cities that are not inhabited, when I bring up the deep (tehom) over you, and the great waters cover you,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tehom" (Strong's H8415, cognate with Akkadian Tiamat) is the primordial deep, the cosmic waters of chaos. Its appearance here is theologically significant — YHWH threatens to reverse creation itself upon Tyre, bringing the pre-creation chaos waters back over the city. This language evokes the Flood narrative and underscores the totality of the judgment. The connection between tehom and the ANE sea-chaos mythology reinforces YHWH's absolute sovereignty over all cosmic forces.

verses.flow: then I will bring you down with those who go down to the Pit, to the people of old, and I will make you to dwell in the land beneath, in the desolations of old, with those who go down to the Pit, so that you will not be inhabited; and I will set glory (tzvi) in the land of the living.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Pit" (bor) is Sheol, the underworld — the realm of the dead in Hebrew cosmology. Ezekiel's oracle places Tyre among "the people of old" (am olam), those long-dead nations who have already descended to the underworld. The phrase "land beneath" (erets tachtiyot) denotes the subterranean underworld realm. The final clause "I will set glory (tzvi) in the land of the living" is textually difficult and may contrast YHWH's honor bestowed on the restored covenant people with Tyre's utter extinction.

verses.flow: I will make you a terror, and you shall be no more; though you be sought, you will never be found again, declares the Lord YHWH."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The declaration "you shall be no more; though you be sought, you will never be found again" anticipates the language of final, irreversible eschatological judgment. Island Tyre was indeed destroyed by Alexander the Great in 332 BC, who used the rubble of mainland Tyre to build a causeway to assault the island — a remarkable fulfillment of the prophecy that her stones and timber would be cast "into the midst of the waters" (v. 12). This passage is often cited in discussions of biblical predictive prophecy and its historical fulfillment (cf. Zech 9:3–4).

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying:
"And you, son of man, lift up a lamentation (qinah) over Tyre,
verses.note: HEBREW: The term qinah (H7015) refers to a formal funeral lament or dirge, often composed in a distinctive 3+2 meter in Hebrew poetry. Its use here is striking — Tyre is being mourned before its fall, as though the prophet is an elegist at a funeral yet to occur. This anticipatory mourning is a prophetic device also seen in Amos 5:1-2 and Ezekiel 19.

verses.flow: and say to Tyre, who dwells at the entrance of the sea, merchant of the peoples to many coastlands: Thus says my Lord YHWH: O Tyre, you yourself have said, 'I am perfect (kalil) in beauty.'
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The boast "I am perfect in beauty" (kalil yophi, H3632+H3308) is the central theological sin of Tyre throughout chapters 26-28 — pride rooted in commercial splendor. The same phrase recurs in Ezekiel 28:12 applied to the king of Tyre, who is described in terms echoing the cherub in Eden, suggesting a spiritual dimension to Tyre's hubris (cf. Isa 14:12-14).

words.g: a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: Your borders are in the heart of the seas; your builders have perfected your beauty.
From the cypresses of Senir they built for you all your planks; they took a cedar from Lebanon to make a mast for you.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Senir (H8149) was the Amorite name for Mount Hermon (Deut 3:9), located in the Anti-Lebanon range. The use of cedar from Lebanon and cypress (or fir) from Senir reflects the well-documented ANE timber trade; Phoenician shipbuilding was legendary in antiquity and is attested in Egyptian, Assyrian, and Greek sources alike.

verses.flow: Of oaks from Bashan they made your oars; they made your deck of ivory inlaid in boxwood, from the coastlands of Cyprus.
Of fine linen (shesh) with embroidery from Egypt was your sail, serving as your banner; blue and purple from the coastlands of Elishah was your awning.
verses.note: HEBREW: Shesh (H8336) refers to fine linen or byssus, a luxury textile associated with Egypt. The detail that Egyptian fine linen served as Tyre's sail or banner (nes, H5251) underscores her international reach — even her maritime display was cosmopolitan. The word "banner" carries a connotation of identity and signal, making the sail itself a declaration of Tyre's glory.

verses.flow: The inhabitants of Sidon and Arvad were your rowers; your skilled men, O Tyre, were in you — they were your pilots.

words.g: the a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: The elders of Gebal and her skilled men were in you, strengthening your seams; all the ships of the sea and their mariners were in you to barter your wares.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Gebal (H1380) is the ancient city known to the Greeks as Byblos, one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world and a major Phoenician center renowned for its craftsmen, scribes, and shipbuilders. The name "Byblos" later gave the Greek language its word for book (biblion), because papyrus was traded there. Its elders strengthening Tyre's ship seams reflects the close kinship of these Phoenician city-states.

verses.flow: Persia and Lud and Put were in your army, your men of war; shield and helmet they hung in you — they gave you your splendor.
The sons of Arvad and your army were upon your walls all around, and valiant men were in your towers; they hung their shields upon your walls all around — they have perfected your beauty.
Tarshish was your merchant because of your great abundance of all kinds of wealth; with silver, iron, tin, and lead they traded for your wares.
Javan, Tubal, and Meshech — they were your merchants; with human lives (nephesh adam) and vessels of bronze they traded your merchandise.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase nephesh adam (H5315+H120) literally means "souls of men" or "lives of human beings," used here for slaves traded as merchandise. This is one of the most explicit condemnations of the slave trade in the Hebrew Bible, and it recurs in Revelation 18:13 in the apocalyptic lament over Babylon, which is patterned directly on this chapter.

verses.flow: From the house of Togarmah — horses and war horses and mules — they traded for your wares.
The sons of Dedan were your merchants; many coastlands were the market of your hand; they brought you as tribute horns of ivory and ebony.
Aram was your merchant because of the multitude of your wares; they traded for your merchandise with emeralds, purple, embroidered work, fine linen, coral, and rubies.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The merchandise list in vv. 12-24 constitutes one of the most extensive catalogs of ancient Near Eastern trade goods in the entire Hebrew Bible, offering extraordinary insight into Late Bronze Age and Iron Age commerce. Tyre served as a hub connecting Mediterranean, Arabian, and Mesopotamian trade networks. Archaeological evidence from Ugarit, Tell el-Ajjul, and other sites confirms the trade in purple dye, textiles, metals, and spices described here.

verses.flow: Judah and the land of Israel — they were your merchants; with wheat of Minnith and Pannag, honey, oil, and balm they traded for your merchandise.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The inclusion of Judah and Israel among Tyre's trading partners is theologically significant — YHWH's own covenant people participated in the commercial web of the very city under divine judgment. Wheat from Minnith and honey and oil recall the bounty of the Promised Land, which was funneled into Gentile commerce rather than consecrated to covenant faithfulness. This detail deepens Ezekiel's indictment of Israel's entanglement with surrounding nations.

verses.flow: Damascus was your merchant because of the multitude of your wares, because of your great abundance of all kinds of wealth — with the wine of Helbon and white wool.
Vedan and Javan from Uzal traded for your wares; wrought iron, cassia, and calamus were among your merchandise.
Dedan was your merchant in saddlecloths for riding.
Arabia and all the princes of Kedar — they were the merchants of your hand; in lambs, rams, and goats — in these they were your merchants.
The merchants of Sheba and Raamah — they were your merchants; with the best of all spices and all precious stones and gold they traded for your wares.
Haran, Canneh, Eden, the merchants of Sheba, Asshur, and Kilmad — they were your merchants.
They were your merchants in choice garments, in garments of blue and embroidered work, and in chests of rich apparel, bound with cords and made of cedar, among your merchandise.

verses.flow: The ships of Tarshish were the caravans for your merchandise; and you were filled and heavily laden in the heart of the seas.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Verse 25 serves as a pivotal hinge in the chapter — the ships of Tarshish, emblematic of Tyre's commercial reach, are praised as her glory before the sudden turn to catastrophe in v. 26. The image of being "filled and heavily laden in the heart of the seas" recalls a ship too burdened to survive the storm, making Tyre's very wealth the instrument of her sinking. This is a classic prophetic irony.

verses.flow: Into great waters your rowers have brought you; the east wind has broken you in the heart of the seas.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "east wind" (ruach qadim, H7307+H6921) is frequently the instrument of divine judgment in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Hos 13:15; Jonah 4:8; Jer 18:17). Here YHWH does not personally destroy Tyre — the east wind does — but the prophetic framing makes clear that the storm is not random but covenantally purposeful. The "heart of the seas," which was Tyre's place of pride (v. 4), becomes her place of ruin.

verses.flow: In their wailing they shall lift up a lamentation over you and lament over you: "Who is like Tyre, like her who is silenced in the midst of the sea?
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The lament "Who is like Tyre?" (mi keTsor) echoes the rhetorical question form used of YHWH Himself — "Who is like YHWH?" (Exod 15:11; Ps 35:10). Tyre's glory had implicitly set itself in competition with divine incomparability. The Revelation 18 lament over Babylon ("What city was like the great city?", Rev 18:18) directly patterns itself on this verse, identifying the eschatological enemy of God's people with Tyre's archetype.

verses.flow: When your wares came from the seas, you satisfied many peoples; with your great wealth and merchandise you enriched the kings of the earth.

verses.flow: Now you are broken by the seas in the depths of the waters; your merchandise and all your assembly in your midst have sunk.
All the inhabitants of the coastlands are appalled over you, and their kings are greatly horrified; their faces are convulsed.
The merchants among the peoples hiss at you; you have become a terror (ballahot), and you shall be no more forever."
verses.note: HEBREW: The word ballahot (H1091) is a strong term meaning terrors, sudden horrors, or objects of dread — used elsewhere for the terror of Sheol (Job 18:14; Ps 88:16). Tyre, once a source of wonder and admiration, becomes herself an object of horror. The closing phrase "you shall be no more forever" (lo tihyeh le'olam) is an absolute negation of permanence — a divine word of final termination standing in stark contrast to the eternity claimed by YHWH alone.

verses.flow: "Son of man, say to the prince of Tyre, Thus says the Lord YHWH: Because your heart has been lifted up, and you have said, 'I am a god, I sit in the seat of gods, in the heart of the seas'; yet you are a man and not a god, though you set your heart as the heart of a god —
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prince of Tyre's claim "I am a god" ('el 'ani) uses the common Hebrew word for deity, not the divine name YHWH. This is a direct usurpation of divine status rooted in human pride, echoing the original sin pattern of Genesis 3. The "seat of gods" (moshav 'elohim) in the heart of the seas reflects Canaanite/Phoenician cosmology in which the divine assembly was situated at the cosmic sea — a claim YHWH now demolishes.

verses.flow: Behold, you are wiser than Daniel; no secret is hidden from you;
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The reference to Daniel here (Hebrew: Dani'el) is likely to the ancient Ugaritic hero Dan'il known from the Ras Shamra texts (14th century BC), a figure proverbially renowned for wisdom and righteousness — paralleling Ezekiel 14:14,20 where Noah, Daniel, and Job are grouped as archetypes of righteousness. This may predate the prophet Daniel, though the biblical Daniel is also not excluded.

verses.flow: "Son of man, lift up a lamentation (qinah) over the king of Tyre, and say to him, Thus says the Lord YHWH: You were the seal of perfection (tokhnit), full of wisdom and perfect in beauty.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Seal of perfection" (khotam toknit, Strong's H8508 tokhnit) — the phrase is unique in the Hebrew Bible. Toknit derives from takhan, meaning "to measure" or "to make proportionate," thus conveying the idea of a measured, complete, and perfect pattern or model. The LXX renders it "seal of likeness." The lamentation genre (qinah) is a 3:2 stressed poetic meter associated with dirges and funeral songs (cf. Lamentations).

words.g: and a gem supposed to be jasper

verses.flow: You were in Eden, the garden of God; every precious stone was your covering — ruby, topaz, and diamond, beryl, onyx, and jasper, lapis lazuli, turquoise, and emerald; and gold, the workmanship of your settings and your engravings, was prepared in you on the day you were created.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Eden, the garden of God" places this being not in the human garden of Genesis 2-3 but in the heavenly archetype — the cosmic garden on the divine mountain. The list of nine precious stones parallels (with variation) the twelve stones of the high priest's breastplate (Exodus 28:17-20), suggesting a priestly-cultic role in the heavenly sanctuary. The phrase "the day you were created" (yom hibbar'ekha) confirms this is a created being, not co-eternal with God.

verses.flow: You were an anointed guardian cherub (kerub); I placed you — you were on the holy mountain of God; in the midst of the stones of fire you walked.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Anointed guardian cherub" (kerub mimshakh hassokek) — the kerub (cherub) in Hebrew theology is not a chubby child but a mighty throne-guardian creature associated with the divine presence (cf. Genesis 3:24; Psalm 18:10; Ezekiel 1; 10). The term "anointed" (mimshakh, from mashakh) implies a formally consecrated role in YHWH's council. "Stones of fire" likely refers to the fiery celestial realm of the divine mountain, comparable to the "sea of glass like crystal" in Revelation 4:6. This verse is pivotal for divine council theology — a high-ranking spiritual being, placed by YHWH himself, who later fell.

verses.flow: You were blameless in your ways from the day you were created, until unrighteousness (avlah) was found in you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Until unrighteousness (avlah) was found in you" — avlah (Strong's H5766) denotes a twisting or perversion of what is straight and just. The passive construction "was found" does not attribute the origin of evil to YHWH but marks the moment of the being's moral deviation. This is one of the most significant Old Testament texts addressing the origin of evil within a created being, paralleling Isaiah 14:12-15 and the NT reflection in John 8:44 and 1 John 3:8.

verses.flow: In the abundance of your trade you were filled with violence (hamas) within, and you sinned; so I cast you as a profane thing from the mountain of God, and I destroyed you, O guardian cherub, from the midst of the stones of fire.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Violence" (hamas, Strong's H2555) — an intense Hebrew term denoting ruthless, destructive wrongdoing that violates social and moral order. It is the same word used to describe the wickedness of the antediluvian world in Genesis 6:11,13. The being's corruption spread from internal pride to outward systemic injustice through commercial excess. "Guardian cherub" appears twice in vv.14,16, forming an inclusion around the fall narrative.

verses.flow: Your heart was lifted up because of your beauty; you corrupted your wisdom for the sake of your splendor. I cast you to the ground; I laid you before kings, to feast their eyes on you.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The fall of this exalted being — cast down from the divine mountain because of pride over beauty — is the cosmic background to the NT's identification of Satan as the original fallen adversary (Luke 10:18; Revelation 12:7-9). The casting "before kings" anticipates the eschatological humiliation of the adversary in Isaiah 14:9-10 and Revelation 20:10. The Messiah's victory is the ultimate reversal of this rebellion.

verses.flow: By the multitude of your iniquities, in the unrighteousness of your trade, you profaned your sanctuaries; so I brought forth fire from your midst — it consumed you, and I turned you to ashes on the earth in the sight of all who see you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Fire from your midst" — the judgment is self-consuming; the very splendor that became an idol is the instrument of destruction. The sanctuaries (miqdashekha, plural) profaned through corrupt trade may refer to the heavenly sanctuary the being once served in — its access to the holy mountain of God now permanently revoked.

verses.flow: and say, Thus says the Lord YHWH: Behold, I am against you, O Sidon, and I will be glorified (nikbadti) in your midst; and they shall know that I am YHWH when I execute judgments in her, and manifest my holiness (nikdashti) in her.
verses.note: HEBREW: Two distinct Hebrew reflexive verbal forms appear in quick succession: nikbadti ("I will be glorified/honored," from kavod, H3519, the weighty glory of God) and nikdashti ("I will show myself holy/sanctified," from qadosh, H6918, the set-apart holiness of God). YHWH's judgment on pagan nations simultaneously reveals both his glory and his holiness — twin pillars of his character throughout Ezekiel (cf. Ezekiel 36:23; 38:23).

verses.flow: Thus says the Lord YHWH: When I gather the house of Israel from the peoples among whom they are scattered, and manifest my holiness in them in the sight of the nations, then they shall dwell in their own land that I gave to my servant Jacob.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The regathering of Israel from among the nations where they were scattered anticipates the New Covenant restoration theme found in Deuteronomy 30:1-10, Jeremiah 31:10, and ultimately the eschatological ingathering of the elect in Matthew 24:31 and Romans 11:25-27. The land promise to "my servant Jacob" grounds the future restoration in the Abrahamic-Patriarchal covenant, which remains operative and unfulfilled apart from a final return.

verses.flow: Speak and say: Thus says the Lord YHWH: Behold, I am against you, Pharaoh king of Egypt, the great dragon (tannin) that lies in the midst of his Nile streams, who has said, 'My Nile is my own; I myself have made it.'
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "tannin" (Strong's H8577) refers to a great serpent or dragon, evoking ancient Near Eastern chaos-monster imagery (cf. Leviathan in Ps 74:13-14; Job 41). Egypt's Pharaoh is cast as a chaos-creature lurking in the Nile, a subversion of Egyptian iconography where the pharaoh was himself identified with divine serpentine power. The claim "I myself have made it" reflects the hubris of self-deification that YHWH systematically judges throughout this section. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The imagery of a chaos dragon being subdued by the divine warrior echoes the cosmic battle motif found across the Psalms (Ps 89:9-10) and ultimately fulfilled in the defeat of the serpent (Rev 12:9).

verses.flow: And I will cast you into the wilderness, you and all the fish of your Nile streams; upon the open field you shall fall — you shall not be gathered or collected. To the beasts of the earth and to the birds of the heavens I have given you as food.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The image of an unburied corpse cast into the open field represents the ultimate covenant curse and shameful death in the ANE worldview — to lie unburied was to be utterly dishonored and without afterlife security. YHWH overturns Egypt's imperial pretensions by reducing Pharaoh to carrion. TYPOLOGY: The language of giving the proud to beasts and birds as food anticipates the eschatological judgment feast of Rev 19:17-21, where the enemies of God are similarly consumed.

verses.flow: When they grasped you with the hand, you splintered and tore all their shoulder; and when they leaned upon you, you broke and caused all their loins to be unsteady.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: This verse likely alludes to specific instances where Israel relied on Egyptian military support — most notably during the reigns of Hoshea (2 Kgs 17:4), Jehoiakim, and Zedekiah — only to find Egypt an unreliable ally that collapsed under Babylonian pressure. The metaphor of a splintered reed-staff was proverbial (cf. 2 Kgs 18:21; Isa 36:6), where an Assyrian envoy used the same image to mock Judah's reliance on Egypt.

verses.flow: therefore, behold, I am against you, and against your Nile streams, and I will make the land of Egypt an utter desolation and waste, from Migdol to Syene, and as far as the border of Cush.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Migdol to Syene" (Hebrew: Migdol to Seveneh) designates the full extent of Egypt from its northeastern border fortress to its southern frontier at the first cataract of the Nile, near modern Aswan. This merism signals total devastation of the entire land. The mention of the border of Cush (Ethiopia/Nubia) extends the scope to Egypt's southernmost territories.

verses.flow: and I will restore the fortunes of Egypt and bring them back to the land of Pathros, to the land of their origin; and there they shall be a lowly kingdom (mamlakah).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Pathros" refers to Upper Egypt (Hebrew: Patros), the southernmost region of Egypt, likely cognate to Egyptian "Pa-to-res" meaning "the Southland." The phrase "land of their origin" (erets mekoram) suggests Pathros as the ancestral homeland of the Egyptians, which aligns with Gen 10:14 where Pathrusim are listed as descendants of Mizraim. THEOLOGICAL: The restoration of Egypt as a "lowly kingdom" after judgment reflects YHWH's pattern of humbling the proud and restoring the contrite — though in diminished form, serving as a perpetual reminder of divine sovereignty over all nations.

verses.flow: And it shall no more be a source of confidence for the house of Israel, recalling their iniquity when they turned to them. Then they shall know that I am the Lord YHWH."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse identifies the root sin for which Egypt is judged in relation to Israel: Israel's repeated turning to Egypt was an act of covenant unfaithfulness, a form of spiritual adultery — trusting in human imperial power rather than YHWH. Egypt's reduction to impotence thus serves a redemptive purpose: removing the temptation that caused Israel to "recall their iniquity." TYPOLOGY: The pattern of YHWH dismantling false sources of security to drive his people back to sole reliance upon himself foreshadows the gospel call to cease trusting in the flesh (Phil 3:3-9).

verses.flow: And it came to pass in the twenty-seventh year, in the first month, on the first day of the month, the word of YHWH came to me, saying:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The date formula — the twenty-seventh year, first month, first day — makes this oracle the latest-dated prophecy in the entire book of Ezekiel (571 BC), placed here thematically rather than chronologically. This demonstrates deliberate editorial structuring by Ezekiel or his school, grouping oracles against Egypt together regardless of their sequential composition date. STRUCTURE: The shift in date creates a dramatic retrospective: Nebuchadrezzar's siege of Tyre (585-573 BC) had just concluded, providing the historical occasion for this compensatory oracle.

verses.flow: "Son of man, Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon made his army labor hard against Tyre; every head was made bald and every shoulder was rubbed bare; yet neither he nor his army got anything from Tyre as wages for the labor he had performed against it.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The siege of Tyre by Nebuchadrezzar (Nebuchadnezzar II) lasted approximately thirteen years (585-573 BC). The mainland city was destroyed, but the island city apparently negotiated terms, leaving Babylon's army exhausted with minimal plunder. The vivid detail of bald heads and rubbed shoulders reflects the physical toll of siege warfare — soldiers carried heavy materials and battering rams for years. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's sovereign direction of Babylonian campaigns as instruments of his judgment (cf. Jer 27:6; Isa 10:5) demonstrates his governance over all nations, not merely Israel.

verses.flow: On that day I will cause a horn (qeren) to spring up for the house of Israel, and I will open your lips among them, and they shall know that I am YHWH."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Horn" (qeren, Strong's H7161) is a standard Hebrew metaphor for strength, power, and royal dignity (cf. Ps 132:17; 1 Sam 2:10). The sprouting of a horn for Israel signals a coming restoration of Davidic royal power — a messianic overtone that culminates in Lk 1:69 where Zechariah uses the identical metaphor ("a horn of salvation") to describe the coming of Jesus from the house of David. TYPOLOGY: The opening of Ezekiel's mouth on "that day" echoes the repeated motif of his dumbness and restored speech (Ezek 3:26-27; 33:22), and the eschatological phrase "on that day" points beyond the immediate Babylonian context to the ultimate Day of YHWH when all nations shall know him.

verses.flow: "Son of man, prophesy and say: Thus says the Lord YHWH: Wail, 'Alas for the day!'
verses.note: HEBREW: "Wail" (yelalah, H3215) is an onomatopoeic cry of distress. The phrase "Alas for the day!" (hah la-yom) is a lament formula signaling imminent catastrophe. The "day of YHWH" invoked here connects this oracle to a broader prophetic motif of divine intervention in history (Amos 5:18-20; Joel 2:1-2).

verses.flow: For near is a day, near is a day of YHWH; a day of cloud, a time of the nations it shall be.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "day of cloud" (yom anan) echoes the theophanic cloud of divine presence turned now to judgment (Exod 19:9; Zeph 1:15). The phrase "a time of the nations" (et goyim) is theologically significant — YHWH exercises sovereign judgment over all nations, not Israel alone, demonstrating his universal kingship.

verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Put" likely refers to Libya; "Lud" to Lydia or Libyan mercenaries; "Cub" is otherwise unattested and debated — some manuscripts read "Libya." The phrase "sons of the land of the covenant" (bene eretz ha-berit) likely refers to Jewish mercenaries or allies in Egypt, perhaps those who fled to Egypt after Jerusalem's fall (Jer 43-44), underscoring the covenant community's tragic entanglement with Egypt.

verses.flow: Thus says the Lord YHWH: And I will destroy the idols (gillulim), and put an end to the worthless images from Noph; and there shall no longer be a prince from the land of Egypt — so I will put fear in the land of Egypt.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Gillulim" (H1544) is a contemptuous term for idols, possibly derived from the word for dung-pellets (gelel), indicating Ezekiel's scorn for Egyptian cult images. "Noph" is Memphis (modern Mit Rahina), cult center of the god Ptah and a major political capital. The cessation of a "prince" (nasi) from Egypt signals a complete collapse of indigenous sovereignty.

verses.flow: "Son of man, I have broken the arm of Pharaoh king of Egypt, and behold, it has not been bound up to be healed, by putting a bandage on it to bind it up, so that it would become strong to grasp the sword.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "broken arm of Pharaoh" refers historically to Pharaoh Hophra's (Apries') failed military intervention to relieve Jerusalem from Babylonian siege (Jer 37:5-8), which ended in Egyptian retreat. The imagery of a broken arm left unbandaged vividly portrays the permanent diminishment of Egyptian military power and YHWH's refusal to allow its recovery.

verses.flow: And I will scatter Egypt among the nations and disperse them among the lands; and they shall know that I am YHWH."

YHWH's purpose throughout is not mere punishment but ultimate acknowledgment of his name across all the earth.

verses.flow: And it came to pass in the eleventh year, in the third month, on the first day of the month, the word of YHWH came to me, saying,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The dating formula places this oracle in the eleventh year of Jehoiachin's exile (587 BC), the third month, first day — approximately two months after the fall of Jerusalem (cf. Ezek 33:21). The oracle is addressed to Pharaoh Hophra (Apries) of Egypt, whose intervention on Judah's behalf had briefly raised hopes during the Babylonian siege (Jer 37:5-11). The timing underscores YHWH's sovereign control over geopolitical events at the moment of Jerusalem's catastrophe.

verses.flow: "Son of man, say to Pharaoh king of Egypt and to his multitude: Whom are you like in your greatness?
Behold, Assyria was a cedar in Lebanon, beautiful of branches and shady of forest and lofty of stature, and its top was among the clouds.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The cedar of Lebanon imagery functions as a cosmic tree archetype — a well-attested motif in ANE literature (Gilgamesh Epic, Tablet V; Ugaritic texts) in which a great tree represents a world-empire straddling heaven and earth. The identification of Assyria as the primary referent serves as a type-scene: as Assyria fell, so shall Egypt. The motif culminates in Dan 4's use of the same image for Nebuchadnezzar, and ultimately finds eschatological reversal in the tree of life (Rev 22:2).

verses.flow: Therefore its height was exalted above all the trees of the field, and its boughs multiplied, and its branches grew long from many waters in its sending them out.
In its boughs all the birds of the heavens made their nests, and under its branches all the beasts of the field gave birth, and in its shade all the great nations dwelt.

verses.flow: The cedars in the garden of God could not hide it; the cypresses were not like its boughs, and the plane trees were not like its branches; no tree in the garden of God was like it in its beauty.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "garden of God" (gan-'elohim, H1588 + H430) echoes the Edenic sanctuary language of Gen 2-3 and points to a primordial sacred space inhabited by divine beings. Comparing earthly kings to trees in the garden of God reflects the ancient cosmological framework in which great empires participate in, or usurp, divine prerogatives. This parallel with Ezek 28:13 (the king of Tyre in Eden) confirms a deliberate literary-theological pattern associating imperial hubris with the original transgression of boundaries between the divine and human realms.

verses.flow: I made it beautiful in the multitude of its branches, and all the trees of Eden that were in the garden of God envied it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The verb "envied" (tiqne'nah, from qana', H7065) used of the trees of Eden is a remarkable personification — jealousy is attributed to the exalted heavenly garden dwellers who witnessed Assyria's incomparable glory. This echoes the competitive dynamics of the divine council (Ps 82; Deut 32:8-9) while simultaneously setting up the irony of the fall: what is most exalted becomes most catastrophically brought low. YHWH alone grants greatness, and what He grants He may withdraw.

verses.flow: I gave it into the hand of the mighty one of the nations; he shall surely deal with it; I have driven it out for its wickedness.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "mighty one of the nations" ('el goyim, H410 + H1471) uses 'el, the generic term for a divine being or powerful ruler, here applied to Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon. This double entendre — simultaneously "mighty one" and carrying overtones of divine agency — reinforces the theological point that Babylon acts as YHWH's instrument of judgment. The verb garash (H1644), "driven out," is the same root used for Adam's expulsion from Eden (Gen 3:24), deepening the Edenic typology of the passage.

verses.flow: And strangers, the most ruthless of nations, cut it down and left it; on the mountains and in all the valleys its branches fell, and its boughs were broken in all the ravines of the land, and all the peoples of the earth descended from its shade and left it.
Upon its fallen trunk all the birds of the heavens will dwell, and upon its branches will be all the beasts of the field,

to the end that no trees beside the waters may exalt themselves in their height, nor set their top among the clouds, nor their mighty ones stand up in their height — all that drink water. For all of them are given over to death, to the lower parts of the earth, in the midst of the sons of man, with those who go down to the pit.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verse 14 draws an explicit theological conclusion from the historical illustration: no earthly power, however great, may claim divine prerogatives. The phrase "lower parts of the earth" (tachtiyyot ha-'aretz, H8482) and "the pit" (bor, H953) frame death as the universal leveler of imperial pride. The address to "all that drink water" universalizes the warning beyond Assyria and Egypt to all nations that draw sustenance from worldly power. This anticipates NT teaching on the humbling of the proud (Luke 1:52; Jas 4:6).

verses.flow: Thus says the Lord YHWH: On the day it went down to Sheol (she'ol) I caused mourning; I covered the deep over it, and I held back its rivers, and the many waters were restrained. I made Lebanon mourn for it, and all the trees of the field languished on account of it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Sheol" (she'ol, H7585) is transliterated here as the Hebrew proper noun for the realm of the dead, the underworld — not merely a synonym for "the grave." The verb "I caused mourning" (he'ebalti, from 'abal, H56) with YHWH as subject is extraordinarily bold: YHWH Himself initiates the cosmic mourning for the fallen empire, demonstrating that even the death of nations is ordered by divine sovereignty. The "deep" (tehom, H8415) being covered recalls creation and flood imagery, suggesting an uncreating judgment.

verses.flow: At the sound of its fall I made the nations quake, when I cast it down to Sheol with those who go down to the pit; and all the trees of Eden, the choicest and best of Lebanon, all that drink water, were comforted in the lower parts of the earth.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "trees of Eden" being "comforted" in the lower parts of the earth (Sheol) at the sound of Assyria's fall evokes the gathering of the shades (rephaim) in the underworld — a concept attested in Ugaritic literature and echoed in Isa 14:9-10, where the shades of kings rise to greet the fallen king of Babylon. The passage envisions a hierarchically ordered underworld populated by the defeated great powers, a cosmological geography consistent with Second Temple Jewish understandings of the cosmos and divine judgment.

verses.flow: Whom are you thus like in glory and in greatness among the trees of Eden? Yet you shall be brought down with the trees of Eden to the lower parts of the earth; in the midst of the uncircumcised you shall lie, with those slain by the sword. This is Pharaoh and all his multitude, declares the Lord YHWH."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The rhetorical question "Whom are you thus like?" returns to frame the entire chapter as a lament-taunt against Pharaoh. The designation "uncircumcised" ('arelim, H6189) is the ultimate shame-label in Hebrew covenant theology, equating Egypt's king with those outside the covenant community entirely. The chapter's Edenic framework — cedar in God's garden, pride, fall, exile to the depths — mirrors the Adam narrative and anticipates the eschatological reversal when the true Davidic King (the Messiah) rules from a restored Eden-temple (Ezek 40-48; Rev 21-22).

verses.flow: "Son of man, lift up a lamentation (qinah) over Pharaoh king of Egypt, and say to him: Like a young lion of the nations you regarded yourself, but you are like the sea-monster (tannin) in the seas; and you thrashed about in your rivers, and you muddied the waters with your feet, and you fouled their rivers."
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "tannin" (Strong's H8577) refers to the great sea-dragon or sea-monster, cognate with the chaos-creature of ANE mythology. In Egyptian iconography the pharaoh was associated with the Nile crocodile (sobek), making this image poignant. The shift from "young lion" to "sea-monster" deflates Egypt's self-image — she is not a noble predator but a chaos creature destined for divine judgment. Compare the use of tannin in Isa 51:9 and Ps 74:13. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The combat myth against chaos (tannin) belongs to the divine-warrior tradition in which YHWH alone subdues the primordial beast, asserting sovereign order over creation.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "net" (reshet) imagery evokes YHWH's role as cosmic hunter and judge, similar to Ezek 12:13 and 17:20, where the same divine trap is set for the king of Israel. The deployment of "many peoples" as agents of judgment reflects YHWH's use of nations as instruments of his covenantal purpose (cf. Hab 1:6, Isa 10:5).

verses.flow: "And when I extinguish you, I will cover the heavens and make their stars dark; I will cover the sun with a cloud, and the moon will not give its light."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The darkening of sun, moon, and stars at Egypt's fall employs cosmological language of de-creation. YHWH's judgment reverses the luminaries established at creation (Gen 1:14-16). TYPOLOGY: This cosmic darkness imagery recurs in Joel 2:10, 3:15 and the Olivet Discourse (Matt 24:29; Mark 13:24), forming a typological pattern fulfilled in the eschatological judgment of world-powers. The darkening at the crucifixion (Luke 23:44-45) may echo this tradition, signaling the judgment of the "ruler of this age."

verses.note: HEBREW: "Gibborim" (Strong's H1368) denotes mighty warriors or champions. The term carries heroic connotations possibly linked to the Nephilim tradition (Gen 6:4).
